title: pr-review-loop: pluggable external-LLM reviewer framework (Google/OpenAI/Anthropic-API/Ollama)
description:
Add a pluggable framework for external-LLM reviewer agents via API tokens (Google, OpenAI, Anthropic-API-direct, etc.) or local endpoints (Ollama). Today our "multi-bot" support is limited to GitHub-hosted bots (Gemini Code Assist, Cursor Bugbot, Claude). Direct-API reviewers let us coordinate genuinely different model families per agent, bypass GH-bot quotas, and support privacy-sensitive repos via local models.

**Treat as a spike per bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`.** Configurable per agent in AGENT-REVIEWERS.md, telemetry-emitting via gx4. We don't know yet which provider/model combinations earn their cost on which kinds of PRs — real-world data via bl7 (replay harness) will tell us.

## Why this is substantial

Three capabilities our current skill (and every peer skill audited) lacks:

1. **Genuine cross-family review diversity.** Anthropic agents + Gemini-Code-Assist-bot share constraints (one is Anthropic-trained, the other is the GH bot's bottleneck). A direct API call to Gemini 2.5 Pro / GPT-4o / Qwen 2.5 Coder with OUR prompt is structurally different — different training data, different RLHF, different failure modes. Model-family diversity ≈ signal diversity.

2. **Cross-family validator for ydy.** The strongest possible version of the independent-validator pipeline (devon-claude-skills-ydy) uses a DIFFERENT model family for the validator than the flagging agent. Anthropic flags → Gemini validates. This is more defensible against agent self-bias than a same-family validator.

3. **Local-model path for privacy-sensitive or air-gapped repos.** Ollama running Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B, DeepSeek Coder, or similar gives teams that can't send code to external APIs a real review option. Zero peer skills offer this.

## Architecture

### Pluggable provider framework

A uniform `ExternalReviewer` interface that wraps each provider's SDK:

```python
class ExternalReviewer:
    name: str
    provider: Literal["google", "openai", "anthropic-api", "ollama", "azure-openai"]
    model: str
    api_key_env: Optional[str]   # env var name; required for hosted providers
    base_url: Optional[str]      # for Ollama / Azure / custom endpoints
    focus: str                   # agent prompt body (same as AGENT-REVIEWERS.md)

    async def review(self, pr_diff: str, changed_files: list[str], context: dict) -> list[Finding]:
        # Send prompt + diff + scoped files to the provider, parse response into Findings
        ...
```

Each external reviewer:
- Receives the same prompt template as native AGENT-REVIEWERS agents (so 5zk category format applies, rb7 confidence + touch-it-you-own-it rules apply, 0nl brainstorm-then-narrow applies)
- Returns findings in our standard `<severity> <category>: <issue>` format
- Posts via the existing comment flow (so 77j pending-review batching applies, vk0 permalinks apply, 08d suggestion blocks apply)
- Emits per-finding telemetry via gx4 with `provider` and `model` fields

### Configuration via AGENT-REVIEWERS.md

Extend the existing `# Agents` section with a new H2 syntax variant:

```markdown
# Agents

## security-reviewer
[standard native subagent definition — uses Claude Code]

## gemini-2.5-security
provider: google
model: gemini-2.5-pro
api_key_env: GOOGLE_API_KEY
focus: |
  Focus on security vulnerabilities: injection, authn/authz, secrets,
  CORS/CSP, dependency CVEs. Apply touch-it-you-own-it rules per rb7.

## gpt-4o-perf
provider: openai
model: gpt-4o
api_key_env: OPENAI_API_KEY
focus: |
  Focus on performance: N+1 queries, algorithm complexity, memory leaks,
  caching opportunities.

## qwen-local
provider: ollama
base_url: http://localhost:11434
model: qwen2.5-coder:32b
focus: |
  Focus on code quality and consistency with existing patterns.
```

Native subagents (Claude Code) and external reviewers (API/local) live side by side. The loop spawns both in parallel; both report findings through the same pipeline.

### Failure handling

- If a provider is unreachable (API down, env var missing, Ollama not running): SKIP that reviewer for the round, log a warning, continue with the rest. Never block the loop.
- Rate limit hit: same as Gemini quota detection — skip and continue. Telemetry records the skip.

## Phased implementation (each phase is a polish-and-upstream unit)

### Phase A — Framework + Google (first upstream-merge candidate)

- Pluggable provider framework (Python module)
- Google Gemini 2.5 Pro integration
- AGENT-REVIEWERS.md syntax extension
- Telemetry integration (gx4)
- Documentation: how to add a provider, how to configure, how API keys flow
- Tests against a mocked Gemini API

This phase is what we polish and propose for upstream merge first. It works standalone — drops into any review skill that supports per-finding agent reviewers. Doesn't require the loop architecture.

### Phase B — More providers (after A merges)

- OpenAI integration (GPT-4o, GPT-5 when available)
- Anthropic API-direct integration (bypasses Claude Code subagent, useful for batch inference and cost control)
- Ollama integration (local models)
- Azure OpenAI integration (corporate users on Azure)
- Cost tracking per provider — log token counts and pricing-estimate per review round
- Per-provider rate-limit handling

### Phase C — Cross-family validator (the moat play)

- ydy validator gains a `validator_provider` config option
- When set, the validator subagent is spawned via the external-LLM framework using a DIFFERENT family than the flagging agent
- Default: flagging Anthropic agent → Anthropic validator (same as ydy today)
- Recommended: flagging Anthropic → Gemini validator (or vice versa)
- Telemetry records the (flagger-family, validator-family) tuple per finding so we can later measure whether cross-family validation catches more real issues than same-family

## Interaction with other tickets

- **AGENT-REVIEWERS.md system** — extends existing config; no breaking changes
- **77j** (pending-review batching) — external-reviewer findings flow through the same batched-review path
- **vk0** (permalinks), **08d** (suggestion blocks), **5zk** (category format) — all apply to external reviewers identically
- **rb7** (touch-it-you-own-it), **0nl** (brainstorm-then-narrow) — same prompt template, applies to external reviewers
- **ydy** (validator) — Phase C extends the validator with cross-family option
- **gx4** (telemetry) — new fields: `provider`, `model`, `provider_latency_ms`, `provider_tokens_used`, `provider_estimated_cost_usd`
- **bl7** (replay harness) — replay can A/B different provider combinations on the same recorded corpus

## Acceptance (Phase A only — for upstream PR)

- New file scripts/external_reviewer.py or similar with pluggable provider framework
- Google Gemini 2.5 Pro provider implementation
- AGENT-REVIEWERS.md parser extended to recognize `provider:` directive
- One worked example in the test fixtures
- Documentation in SKILL.md (or references/external-reviewers.md per the references-pattern refactor)
- Failure modes documented and handled gracefully
- Telemetry emits provider/model fields per finding
- README updated explaining configuration and adding new providers

## Open questions

- API key management: env vars per provider, or a unified `~/.config/pr-review-loop/keys.env`? Env vars are simpler and standard.
- Should we ship default external-reviewer entries (e.g. "if you have GOOGLE_API_KEY set, automatically add a Gemini security-reviewer alongside the native one") or always require explicit AGENT-REVIEWERS.md opt-in? Probably explicit opt-in — autoinstalling external reviewers raises cost and privacy expectations.
- Cost guards: should the loop hard-stop if estimated provider cost exceeds a budget? Reasonable feature for Phase B; not blocking.
- Conflict resolution: if a native subagent and an external reviewer flag the same finding, do we dedupe? Probably yes — extend the dedup logic that 77j already proposes.

title: Epic: pr-review-loop ← MiniMax-AI pr-review skill (two-phase model) learnings
description:
Harvest the two-phase model (deterministic pre-checks → LLM content review) from MiniMax-AI's pr-review skill (https://github.com/MiniMax-AI/skills/tree/main/.claude/skills/pr-review) for our pr-review-loop.

## Read of the upstream

Their skill is meta — it reviews submissions to their skills repo, NOT application code PRs. Most of the content (kebab-case dir names, SKILL.md frontmatter validation, README sync, license declaration) doesn't transfer. But the architectural pattern is the most useful new idea since the validation pipeline in 0cp:

1. **Phase 1: Automated Validation (Hard Rules)** — zero-dep Python validator runs deterministic checks (regex secret scan + structural rules) BEFORE the LLM review phase. Severity-coded: ERROR blocks, WARN flags.
2. **Phase 2: Content Review (Soft Guidelines)** — LLM-driven review against quality guidelines, only after Phase 1 passes.

The principle: front-load cheap deterministic checks so expensive LLM agents don't waste tokens analyzing code with issues a regex would catch.

## What we are taking

- **devon-claude-skills-dl6** (P2, spike): deterministic pre-checks before round 1 — regex secret scanner (always on), project linter (auto-detect, opt-out), type-checker (auto-detect, opt-out), PR title format (opt-in), test runner (opt-in). Severity-coded ERROR/WARN. ERRORs block round 1 (auto-fix trivial, ask user on non-trivial); WARNs feed agents as "linter has already flagged these — don't re-raise."

## Refinements to existing tickets

- **9ao (security-reviewer)** — defer high-confidence secret patterns (sk-, AKIA, Bearer JWT) to dl6's deterministic pre-check; agent focuses on context-sensitive security (validation gaps, auth logic, CORS/CSP, dep CVEs).
- **v7h (pre-flight skip)** — sequenced with dl6: v7h decides whether to review at all → dl6 decides what to deterministically fix first → vsf decides autonomous vs advisory → round 1 starts.

## Philosophy check — none

Their hard rules are about *artifact structure*, not *what scope of findings to flag*. Touch-it-you-own-it untouched.

## Skip — not applicable

- Skill-repo-specific structural rules (kebab-case, frontmatter validation, README sync, license declaration) — we're not reviewing skill submissions
- "One PR, one purpose" rule — too LLM-judgmenty to enforce cheaply; could fold into agent prompts but probably noise
- `references/` pattern as a structural refactor of our SKILL.md — good hygiene we can apply anytime, not a peer-skill-learning ticket

## Spike framing

Per bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`, dl6 is configurable and telemetry-emitting. We don't know yet whether pre-checks save real rounds or just shift work; gx4 telemetry will measure the overlap between pre-check findings and agent findings, and bl7 (replay harness) can A/B with and without pre-checks on the same corpus.

## Sequencing in the loop

Combined with sibling tickets, the loop entry becomes:

1. **v7h** (pre-flight skip) — should we review at all?
2. **dl6** (deterministic pre-checks) — what to deterministically fix first?
3. **vsf** (sensitive-area gate) — autonomous or advisory mode?
4. Round 1 agent spawning
5. **3z8** (incremental round mode) — round 2+ scopes to commits since prior bookmark

title: pr-review-loop: deterministic pre-checks (secret scan + linter + typecheck) before round 1
description:
Before spawning agent reviewers in round 1, run cheap deterministic checks (regex secret scan, project linter, type-checker, PR title format). Front-load these so expensive LLM agents don't waste tokens analyzing code that has issues a regex could catch.

**Treat as a spike per bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`.** Configurable on/off, with telemetry (gx4) measuring whether pre-checks reduce per-round findings or duplicate them.

## Why

Right now our loop spawns agents in round 1 immediately. If the PR has a leaked API key, the security-reviewer agent (9ao) will eventually catch it via judgment — but a regex would catch it in 50ms with zero false negatives for the high-confidence patterns. If the project has a linter and the PR has lint failures, we currently catch those in the POST-push CI fix loop, after we've already spent agent tokens on a round of review.

Both cases waste rounds. Front-loading cheap deterministic checks before round 1 means agents start with cleaner input.

## Reference

MiniMax-AI/skills `.claude/skills/pr-review/scripts/validate_skills.py` runs zero-dep deterministic checks BEFORE the LLM content-review phase. Their checks are skill-repo-specific (frontmatter, dir names) but the **two-phase model** is the takeaway.

## What to build

### Pre-check pipeline

scripts/precheck.sh runs the following against the PR diff:

1. **Secret scanner** (always on) — 3+ high-confidence regex patterns:
   - `sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{20,}` (OpenAI-style)
   - `AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}` (AWS access key)
   - `Bearer\s+[a-zA-Z0-9_\-\.]{50,}` (JWT)
   - Extensible: project can add patterns via `.claude/pr-review-precheck-patterns.txt`
   - Result: ERROR per match → block round 1 until resolved

2. **Project linter** (auto-detect, opt-out) — run whatever lint command is configured:
   - Detect: `.eslintrc*` → `eslint`, `pyproject.toml` ruff config → `ruff check`, `.rubocop.yml` → `rubocop`, etc.
   - Result: WARN findings get fed to round 1 agents as pre-existing context ("the linter already flagged these — do not re-flag"). ERROR findings block.

3. **Type-checker** (auto-detect, opt-out) — run if a typed-language project:
   - `tsconfig.json` → `tsc --noEmit`
   - `pyproject.toml` with mypy/pyright config → run it
   - `go.mod` → `go vet` + `go build`
   - Result: type errors are usually ERROR-level (won't compile = high-signal per rb7). Block round 1.

4. **PR title format** (opt-in) — regex against conventional-commits or repo's documented format. WARN only.

5. **Test runner** (opt-in, expensive) — only if user passes `--run-tests`. Otherwise leave to CI.

### Severity model (from MiniMax-AI)

- **ERROR** — block round 1 entirely. Report to user, ask to fix (or auto-fix if trivial like format-on-save), then retry.
- **WARN** — don't block; pass to round 1 agents as known context so they don't re-raise.

### Integration with the loop

- Runs once at loop start, before any agent spawning
- Reports findings to user
- If ERRORs exist:
  - Auto-fix mode (default for trivial formatters): fix, commit, push (via commit-and-push.sh), retry pre-check
  - Stop-and-ask mode (for secrets, type errors, lint errors that need code changes): pause, surface findings, wait for user
- WARN findings injected into agent-spawn prompt as "linter has already flagged these — focus your review elsewhere"

## Interaction with other tickets

- **9ao (security-reviewer)** — overlap on secrets. Pre-check handles high-confidence regex patterns; agent focuses on context-sensitive security (e.g. "this validation is missing for THIS user input"). Append note to 9ao.
- **v7h (pre-flight skip)** — adjacent. v7h decides *whether* to review at all. This decides *what to deterministically fix first*. Sequential: v7h runs first; if proceed, then this. Cross-reference.
- **vsf (sensitive-area gate)** — also adjacent. vsf checks if the PR is *safe to auto-iterate on*. This pre-check fixes deterministic-tooling issues regardless of sensitivity.
- **rb7 (do-not-flag list)** — already says "issues a linter will catch — do NOT flag (and do NOT run the linter to verify)." This ticket explicitly RUNS the linter once at pre-check time, then injects the WARN findings as context. Reconciles cleanly: agents still don't run linters per-finding; pre-check runs it once and shares the result.
- **gx4 (telemetry)** — emits per-finding records with `source: precheck-secret-scan | precheck-linter | precheck-typecheck | agent-review` so we can measure how often pre-check beats agents to a finding.

## Configuration

```yaml
precheck:
  secret_scan: true       # always on
  linter:
    enabled: auto         # auto | on | off
    block_on_error: true
  typecheck:
    enabled: auto
    block_on_error: true
  pr_title_format: off    # regex string when enabled
  run_tests: false        # opt-in expensive check
```

## Acceptance

- scripts/precheck.sh exists, runs all enabled checks, exits with severity-coded status
- Loop calls pre-check before round 1, blocks/auto-fixes/passes appropriately
- WARN findings reach agent reviewers as "known issues, don't re-raise"
- Telemetry (gx4) captures pre-check findings separately from agent findings so the eval script (gx4 + bl7) can measure overlap

## Out of scope

- Replacing CI entirely — CI still runs post-push. Pre-check is opportunistic, not authoritative.
- Custom check plugins beyond regex patterns — start with the canonical tooling list (eslint/ruff/tsc/mypy/etc.); extension model can come later.

title: Epic: pr-review-loop ← udecode reviewing-pr skill (via mcpmarket comprehensive-pr-reviewer) learnings
description:
Harvest the one genuinely new idea from udecode/dotai's reviewing-pr skill (re-published on mcpmarket.com as `comprehensive-pr-reviewer` under a third-party handle; see bd memory `provenance-comprehensive-pr-reviewer` for the full provenance trail).

## Read of the upstream

Architecturally the simplest of the six peer skills audited: single SKILL.md, ~285 lines, pure prompt, no `allowed-tools`, no sub-agents, no scripts, no references directory. Closest sibling is aidankinzett (similar shape, but aidankinzett is materially better: pending-review batching + explicit approval gate + tools allowlist).

Notable for **one feature that no other peer had**: an `incremental` parameter that scopes review to the latest commit's diff (`HEAD~1..HEAD`) rather than the full PR diff.

## What we are taking

- **devon-claude-skills-3z8** (P2, spike): incremental round mode — review only commits added since the prior round, with full-diff fallback for round 1, `--full` override, sensitive-area escalations, and the "one more loop" final-verification round.

## Refinements to existing tickets

- **5zk** (category-prefix format) — extended with the orthogonal severity-emoji dimension (🚨 / ⚠️ / 📝 / 💡) for a combined `<emoji> <category>: <issue>` format
- **77j** (pending-review batching) — extended with two HEREDOC body templates (full-diff vs incremental) so round-over-round tracking is visible to human PR readers

## Philosophy conflict — none

Their severity tiers describe WHAT to flag, not WHEN. No "skip pre-existing" or "skip general quality" language. Aligned by default with our touch-it-you-own-it stance.

## Skip — already covered or already do better

- Severity tiers in summary output → already in zbn via sontek's template
- "Pull existing comments first" anti-duplication → our loop already does this
- Auto-action via `gh pr review --approve/--request-changes` → already configurable in 77j via event-policy presets (per presmihaylov refinement)
- No `allowed-tools` frontmatter → already covered by oma
- Single megacomment / no sub-agents / no CI awareness → we already do better
- Branded trailer → already in post-line-comment.sh

## Provenance note

This is a third-hand re-publish. udecode (true author) deleted the skill from dotai on 2026-01-21. The mcpmarket listing serves a snapshot of a now-deleted skill, under a different attribution. Cite udecode SHA `d3d9524b40` as the canonical source, not the mcpmarket page or Activer007 aggregator. See bd memory `provenance-comprehensive-pr-reviewer`.

## Spike framing

Per bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`, the incremental round mode is a hypothesis to validate. Real-world telemetry via gx4 plus replay comparison via bl7 will tell us whether incremental mode saves tokens without dropping signal. Implement configurable; default on for rounds 2+ but easy to disable.

title: pr-review-loop: incremental round mode — review only commits since last round
description:
After the first round of the autonomous loop reviews the full PR diff, subsequent rounds should default to reviewing only commits added since the prior round, not the full PR diff again.

**Treat as a spike per bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`.** Configurable on/off, telemetry-emitting (per gx4) so we can measure whether incremental mode actually improves signal or accidentally drops findings.

## The problem this solves

Current loop behavior: every round, agents see the full PR diff. After round 1's fixes land and round 2 begins, agents re-analyze unchanged code. This wastes tokens, risks re-flagging already-triaged issues ("Won't fix" in round N → re-raised in round N+1), and dilutes the signal — the genuinely interesting work in round N+1 is what changed in response to round N's feedback.

## Reference pattern

From udecode/dotai pre-deletion SHA `d3d9524b40` (re-published on mcpmarket as `comprehensive-pr-reviewer`; see bd memory `provenance-comprehensive-pr-reviewer`):

> The skill includes an `incremental` parameter that, when set to true, focuses exclusively on the diff of the latest commit.

The udecode skill applies it to a single commit boundary. We can generalize to `commits-since-prior-round..HEAD`.

## What to build

1. **Round-bookmark tracking**: at the end of each round, persist the current HEAD SHA (e.g. in `.beads/pr-review-loop/<pr>/rounds.jsonl`). Telemetry (gx4) already wants per-round records; piggyback.

2. **Round-N+1 diff scope**: when starting round N+1, compute the diff range as `bookmark[N]..HEAD`. Pass this to all agent reviewers as their analysis target instead of `base..HEAD`.

3. **Full-diff fallback** — always available for:
   - Round 1 (no bookmark yet)
   - Any round explicitly requested with `--full` flag
   - Sensitive-area escalations (per vsf) — when sensitive code is touched, force full-diff re-review

4. **Findings carry forward**: agents should still see existing comments from prior rounds (we already do this) so they can verify earlier findings were addressed. They just don't re-analyze the underlying code unless it changed.

5. **Final verification round**: when entering the "one more loop" final-verification round (existing SKILL.md behavior around L196), use full diff. This catches anything the incremental rounds missed.

## Risks to measure via gx4 telemetry

- **Missed findings**: an incremental round might miss a real issue in code that wasn't touched but is now broken by an unrelated fix elsewhere. Telemetry should record whether ANY round (incremental or full) flagged each finding; gap analysis tells us if incremental mode is dropping signal.
- **Stale assumptions**: agent's mental model of the broader code may degrade if it never re-sees unchanged files across rounds. Mitigation: agents can still Read referenced files; we just narrow the diff they're told to analyze.
- **Cross-file ripple effects**: a fix in file A may break file B without modifying B. Incremental mode would miss this. Full-diff final verification (item 5) is the safety net.

## Configuration

```yaml
round_scope:
  mode: incremental_default   # or: full_always
  final_verification: full    # always full regardless of mode
  on_sensitive: full          # override when vsf flags sensitive areas
```

## Interaction with other tickets

- **gx4 (filter telemetry)** — adds `round_scope` and `diff_range` fields per finding so we can compare incremental vs full-diff rounds
- **bl7 (replay test harness)** — replay can re-run a recorded PR under either mode to measure signal delta on the same input
- **vsf (sensitive-area gate)** — sensitive PRs force full-diff
- **77j (pending-review batching)** — orthogonal; incremental round still posts a batched review per agent
- **rb7 / ydy / 0nl (filters)** — apply the same way regardless of diff scope; just operate on a smaller candidate set in incremental mode

## Acceptance

- Round-bookmark SHA persisted per round, readable on next round start
- Incremental mode is the default for rounds 2+, with full-diff override available
- Final verification round always uses full diff
- Sensitive-area escalations force full diff
- Telemetry captures the scope used per round
- Replay harness (bl7) can A/B incremental vs full on the same recorded corpus

title: pr-review-loop: configurable filter stack + telemetry for real-world evaluation of rb7/ydy/0nl
description:
Make the three filter approaches (rb7 confidence threshold, 0nl brainstorm-then-narrow, ydy independent validator) configurable and emit structured telemetry so real-world usage can tell us which earn their cost in which combinations.

## Why this matters

These three filters are competing hypotheses, not complementary by construction:

- **rb7 (confidence threshold + touch-it-you-own-it)** — runs INSIDE the flagging agent; effectively free; catches findings the agent itself doubts
- **0nl (brainstorm 5–7 → narrow to 1–2)** — runs INSIDE the flagging agent; cheap (more reasoning, same agent); catches tunnel vision on the first issue spotted
- **ydy (independent validator subagent per finding)** — runs AFTER flagging; expensive (N extra subagents per round); catches agent self-bias (rationalizing marginal cases up over threshold)

We don't actually know which of these is worth running in which combinations on which kinds of PRs. A confidence threshold might be enough alone. A validator might subsume the threshold. Brainstorming might only help on broad-checklist agents (security, performance) and add noise to narrow ones. We will only learn from real usage.

## What to build

### 1. Config-driven filter stack

Add a section to SKILL.md (or a sibling config file) that lets the user toggle each filter and tune its parameters. Default ON for cheap filters (rb7 + 0nl), default OFF for expensive ones (ydy) until evaluated:

```yaml
filters:
  confidence_threshold:
    enabled: true
    threshold: 80          # 0-100
  brainstorm_narrow:
    enabled: true
    initial_count: 5       # range bound: 5-7
    narrowed_count: 1      # range bound: 1-2
  independent_validator:
    enabled: false         # off by default until we have data
    model:
      bug: opus
      claudemd: sonnet
```

Per-agent overrides allowed (e.g. security-reviewer always uses validator; comment-analyzer never does).

### 2. Per-finding telemetry

For each finding the loop processes, emit a structured log line capturing:

- finding_id, agent_name, file, line, severity, raw_text
- which filters were applied, what each filter said (passed / dropped / confidence-score / valid-invalid-uncertain)
- final disposition: posted / dropped-by-filter-X / suppressed-as-duplicate
- after the round closes: was this finding addressed (Fixed), rejected by user/author (Won't fix), captured to beads (Out of scope), or never resolved?

Format: JSONL, one line per finding, stored under .beads/pr-review-loop/findings/<pr>-<round>.jsonl or similar. Should be cheap to parse later.

### 3. Per-round/per-loop aggregation

At end of each round and end of each loop, emit aggregate metrics:

- Findings raw (pre-filter) per agent
- Findings posted (post-filter) per agent
- Per-filter retention rate (% passed)
- Per-agent final outcome distribution (Fixed / Won't fix / Out of scope / Unresolved)
- Round time and total subagent token cost (rough estimate)

### 4. Evaluation script

scripts/analyze-filter-effectiveness.sh that ingests a corpus of JSONL files (across many PRs/rounds) and outputs:

- Which filters drop findings that would have been "Won't fix" (good — filter is doing its job)
- Which filters drop findings that would have been "Fixed" (bad — filter is too aggressive)
- Cost vs. benefit per filter (estimated token cost vs. reduction in human review burden)
- Per-agent profiles: which filters help which agents

## Acceptance

- All three filters are toggleable and parameter-tunable via config (not hardcoded in SKILL.md)
- Every finding generates a JSONL telemetry record
- Aggregate metrics print at end of round and end of loop
- An evaluation script exists that can ingest >= 10 PRs of telemetry and produce a per-filter report
- Default config is documented with rationale (which are ON/OFF by default and why)

## Open questions for design

- Storage location for JSONL — under .beads/, under the repo, or in a user-level cache?
- Should the loop auto-share telemetry back to a central place (opt-in)? Faster cross-user evaluation, but privacy implications
- What's the minimum corpus size before per-filter conclusions are meaningful? (Probably 50+ PRs, varies by filter)

## Relation to other tickets

- **rb7, ydy, 0nl** — each must be implemented so it can be toggled and so it emits the telemetry fields this framework expects. Append a note to each pointing here.
- **bfd / 9ao** — per-agent overrides land naturally on the native subagent frontmatter (per oma).

title: Epic: pr-review-loop ← sontek Code Review Expert skill learnings
description:
Bring useful patterns and checklist content from sontek's "Code Review Expert" skill (https://mcpmarket.com/tools/skills/code-review-expert-8) into pr-review-loop.

## Read of the upstream

Simplest of the four peer skills we've reviewed: a single SKILL.md, no commands, no scripts, no plugin manifest. Single-shot review (gather → review → emit structured Markdown). The architectural surface is minimal — the VALUE is in the CONTENT of the checklist, which is the most thorough of any peer skill.

## What we are taking

- **devon-claude-skills-9ao** (P2): add `security-reviewer` and `performance-reviewer` to defaults — fills a real gap in bfd (the 6 from anthropics/pr-review-toolkit don't cover either). Sontek's checklists are the source material.
- **devon-claude-skills-0nl** (P3): brainstorm-then-narrow ("list 5-7 concerns, narrow to 1-2") investigation pattern in the agent-spawn prompt. Complements rb7 and ydy as a third filter layer that shapes WHAT the agent considers (not just what it posts).
- **devon-claude-skills-vsf** (P3): sensitive-area escalation gate — pause loop and ask user when PR touches DB schema/migrations, auth, API contracts, infra, framework adoption, or other "needs human judgment" categories. From sontek's "Long-Term Considerations" list.

## Refinements to existing tickets

- **bfd** — extended with per-specialist checklist content mapping (sontek's checklist → which default agent gets which slice). Also calls out what NOT to copy from sontek (approval decisions, long-term considerations which go to vsf instead).
- **zbn** — extended with sontek's detailed output template (Overall Assessment / Key Changes / Critical / Important / Suggestions / Positive / Testing / Recommendation), adapted to our merge-readiness summary.

## What we explicitly skip

- Single-shot "Approve / Request Changes / Comment" decision — our loop doesn't decide approval; we iterate until convergence and let CI/branch protection gate merge.
- Self-imposed "always assume good intent, be respectful" tone instructions — fine in principle, low value for AI-to-AI comments.

## Philosophy check

No conflict with touch-it-you-own-it (rb7 + bd memory). Sontek's checklist treats test coverage as a blocking concern (aligned), has no "skip pre-existing" language, and his "Don't block for" list is just nitpicks/perfectionism — same as ours.

## Suggested order

1. **bfd** (already in queue from gnp epic) — ships the 6 baseline defaults
2. **9ao** — adds security + performance to make 8 defaults (blocks on bfd)
3. **0nl** — small spawn-prompt tweak; can land standalone
4. **vsf** — independent feature; coordinate with v7h (pre-flight skip) so they compose properly
5. **zbn** (already in queue from gnp epic) — picks up the detailed output template refinement

title: pr-review-loop: add security-reviewer + performance-reviewer to default specialists
description:
Add two more native specialist subagents to the shipped defaults: `security-reviewer` and `performance-reviewer`. Neither focus area is covered by the 6 agents we're planning to ship via devon-claude-skills-bfd (which come from the upstream pr-review-toolkit).

## Why

The 6 defaults from anthropics/claude-plugins-official pr-review-toolkit cover:
- CLAUDE.md compliance (code-reviewer)
- error handling/silent failures (silent-failure-hunter)
- test coverage (pr-test-analyzer)
- comment rot (comment-analyzer)
- type design (type-design-analyzer)
- simplification (code-simplifier)

NO security-specific or performance-specific specialist. The pr-test-analyzer touches some of this incidentally; code-reviewer can flag CLAUDE.md-mandated security rules; but real audit-grade coverage of injection, authn/authz, secrets, dependency CVEs, N+1 queries, indexing, complexity, etc. is missing.

Sontek's "Code Review Expert" skill at https://mcpmarket.com/tools/skills/code-review-expert-8 covers both in detailed checklists worth using as source material.

## security-reviewer focus areas

(from sontek's Security checklist)

- Injection: SQL injection, command injection, XSS, path traversal
- Authentication & Authorization: permission checks, role validation
- Data exposure: sensitive data in logs, error messages, API responses
- Input validation: sanitize/validate all user inputs
- Secrets management: no hardcoded credentials/API keys/tokens
- Dependency vulnerabilities: known CVEs in new dependencies
- CORS & CSP: proper configuration for web applications

Model: opus (security needs strong reasoning).

## performance-reviewer focus areas

(from sontek's Performance checklist)

- Algorithm complexity: O(n²) or worse where O(n)/O(log n) is possible
- Database queries: N+1 problems (missing prefetch/select_related), missing indexes for new query patterns, unbounded queries without pagination, inefficient joins/subqueries
- Memory usage: unnecessary data copying, memory leaks, large allocations
- Caching: opportunities for caching expensive operations
- Network calls: batching, unnecessary requests, missing timeouts

Model: opus.

## What to build

1. Author plugins/pr-review-loop/agents/security-reviewer.md and performance-reviewer.md as native Claude Code subagents (per devon-claude-skills-oma — name/description/model/color frontmatter)
2. Include them in the default set spawned by the loop (the same mechanism that ships the other 6)
3. Apply the touch-it-you-own-it philosophy in both prompts (rb7) — issues in TOUCHED files are in scope, pre-existing or otherwise; trivial → fix, non-trivial → beads
4. Each agent's body should include its checklist content as scoped guidance, with examples of what to flag and what not to flag

## Acceptance

- Two new native subagents ship alongside the 6 from bfd
- Default set spawns 8 agents (or whatever subset the user customizes via AGENT-REVIEWERS.md)
- Each agent prompts agents to focus on its specialty — security-reviewer doesn't flag perf, performance-reviewer doesn't flag injection

## Defer high-confidence secret detection to pre-check (from MiniMax-AI review)

devon-claude-skills-dl6 introduces deterministic pre-checks before round 1, including a regex-based secret scanner for high-confidence patterns (OpenAI `sk-`, AWS `AKIA`, Bearer JWTs).

This agent should NOT re-do that work. Specifically:

- **Skip flagging**: exact-match high-confidence secret patterns already covered by the pre-check regex set (they'll have already been blocked or flagged before this agent runs)
- **DO flag**: context-sensitive security issues that need agent judgment — missing validation for a specific user input path, authn/authz logic gaps, vague-but-real injection vectors that aren't simple regex matches, dependency CVEs in newly added packages, CORS/CSP misconfigurations, etc.

The pre-check runs the cheap stuff once. The agent focuses on what requires judgment.

title: Epic: pr-review-loop ← anthropics/claude-code code-review plugin (Boris Cherny) learnings
description:
Bring pipeline-design patterns from anthropics/claude-code plugins/code-review (Boris Cherny) into pr-review-loop.

## Read of the upstream

Single-pass review command (no loop), 4 parallel reviewer agents + per-finding validator subagents, confidence-filtered output to terminal or PR comment. The pipeline design is excellent — several patterns are structurally stronger than what's already in our queue and worth adopting outright.

## What we are taking

- **Two-stage validation** (devon-claude-skills-ydy, P2) — biggest idea. Independent subagent validates each finding before posting. Stronger than confidence threshold inside the flagging agent because the validator does not share the original agent's bias.

- **Pre-flight skip** (devon-claude-skills-v7h, P3) — cheap haiku check at loop start that short-circuits on draft/closed/trivial PRs.

- **Model-tiered dispatch** (devon-claude-skills-0np, P3) — haiku for pre-flight, sonnet for compliance, opus for bug-class and validation. Per-agent model selection.

- **GitHub permalinks in comments** (devon-claude-skills-vk0, P3) — full SHA + line range + ≥1 line context, in the format that renders correctly in inline comment markdown.

- **MCP inline-comment server investigation** (devon-claude-skills-ypj, P3) — could replace some/all of our gh-api scripts if publicly usable.

- **Refinements to existing tickets:**
  - rb7 (confidence threshold) — extended with the sharper do-not-flag list and HIGH SIGNAL criteria, including the "quote the exact CLAUDE.md rule" forcing function
  - 08d (suggestion blocks) — extended with "only suggest if commit fully fixes" and "no suggestion blocks for 6+ line fixes"

## What we explicitly skip

- "Stop if Claude already reviewed this PR" — anti-pattern for our autonomous loop; prior Claude activity is the point
- Single-shot architecture — our loop is the differentiator

## Suggested order

1. **ydy** (two-stage validation) — the structural change; do this first since it's the foundation for trust in agent output
2. **v7h** (pre-flight skip) — small standalone win
3. **vk0** (permalinks) — small standalone win; touches the same post-comment surface as 77j
4. **0np** (model tiering) — easier after devon-claude-skills-oma + bfd land
5. **ypj** (MCP investigation) — research, then either kill or supersede 77j

## Cross-epic notes

- This work overlaps with the devon-claude-skills-gnp epic (pr-review-toolkit learnings) on:
  - Confidence scoring (rb7) — refined by both epics
  - Suggestion blocks (08d, in the aidan epic devon-claude-skills-37s) — refined by both
- And with devon-claude-skills-37s (aidankinzett epic) on:
  - 77j (pending review batching) — the ypj MCP investigation could supersede it
  - 08d (suggestion blocks) — refined here

title: pr-review-loop: two-stage validation — independent subagent verifies each finding before posting
description:
Add a second-pass validation stage where each finding from agent reviewers is independently verified by a separate subagent (no context of the original flag) before being posted.

## Why

Our current rb7 ticket plans to filter findings via a confidence threshold (>=80) inside the FLAGGING agent's prompt. That's better than nothing but the flagging agent has bias toward its own findings — it can rationalize a marginal case up over the threshold.

Boris Cherny's anthropics/claude-code code-review plugin uses a different pattern that is structurally stronger: after the parallel review agents emit findings, spawn ONE validator subagent PER FINDING. The validator gets the PR title/description and the issue description, but does NOT know which agent flagged it or why they were confident. It re-checks the claim against the actual code. Only findings that survive validation are posted.

This catches bias the original agent can't see in itself.

## What to build

1. **After step 6 of each round** (agent reviewers complete), but BEFORE posting comments:
   - For each finding, spawn a validator Task in parallel
   - Validator prompt template: "You are validating issue X claimed in file Y line Z. Verify against the actual code. Return: VALID / INVALID / UNCERTAIN with one-line reason."
   - Model selection: opus for bug/logic findings, sonnet for CLAUDE.md compliance findings (matches Boris's pattern)
   - Validator does NOT have access to the original agent's name or rationale

2. **Filter** findings to only VALID survivors, then proceed with the existing post-comment flow.

3. **Track validation rejection rate** per agent. If an agent's findings are rejected >50% in a round, that's a strong retire signal (extends our existing diminishing-returns tracking).

## Interaction with other tickets

- **rb7** (confidence threshold) — keep as a first-pass filter inside the flagging agent. This new stage runs AFTER. Belt-and-suspenders.
- **77j** (pending review batching) — validation runs BEFORE the pending review is built. Filtered set goes into the batched review.
- **oma** (native subagents) — validators can be a separate native subagent (e.g. "finding-validator-bug", "finding-validator-claudemd") with proper model selection.

## Reference

anthropics/claude-code plugins/code-review/commands/code-review.md, step 5:

> For each issue found in the previous step by agents 3 and 4, launch parallel subagents to validate the issue. These subagents should get the PR title and description along with a description of the issue. The agent's job is to review the issue to validate that the stated issue is truly an issue with high confidence ... Use Opus subagents for bugs and logic issues, and sonnet agents for CLAUDE.md violations.

## Acceptance

- Validation stage exists between agent flagging and comment posting
- Validator subagents run in parallel
- Per-agent rejection rate is tracked (used to inform retirement)
- Loop logs show count of findings flagged vs validated vs posted per round

## Validators rule on REALNESS, not SCOPE

The validator's job is to confirm the claim against the actual code: "is this issue real?" — NOT "is this issue in scope for this PR?"

A pre-existing bug is still a real bug. A weak-test-coverage gap on a touched code path is still a real gap. Under our touch-it-you-own-it philosophy (devon-claude-skills-rb7, bd memory `code-review-philosophy-when-you-touch-code-you`), both are in scope when the file is in the PR's diff.

The validator prompt MUST therefore NOT include reasoning hooks like:
- "Was this issue introduced by this PR?"
- "Is this a general quality concern that should be deferred?"
- "Does CLAUDE.md require this?"

Instead, the validator prompt asks ONLY:
- "Does the issue exist in the code as described?" (VALID/INVALID/UNCERTAIN)
- "If VALID, is the severity claim plausible?"

Scope filtering (touched vs untouched, in-scope vs beads-followup) is the flagging agent's responsibility, applied before findings reach the validator. The validator stays narrow.

## Treat this as a configurable hypothesis, not baked-in policy

Per devon-claude-skills-gx4, this filter must be:
- Toggleable via config (on/off)
- Parameter-tunable (threshold value / brainstorm counts / validator model selection, as applicable)
- Telemetry-emitting per the JSONL schema in that ticket

We don't know yet whether this filter earns its cost in which combinations with the other two. Real-world usage will tell us. Implement it to *measure*, not to assert.


---

## Acceptance addition: model-tiered validators (folds in 0np)

The validator's model MUST be selected by the FLAGGING agent's category, not by the flagger's own model. This bakes 0np's per-task model-tiering into ydy from day one (no refactor later).

| Flagging agent type | Validator model |
|--------------------|-----------------|
| Bug-class (silent-failure-hunter, pr-test-analyzer, code-simplifier, security-reviewer when shipped via 9ao, performance-reviewer when shipped via 9ao) | **opus** — bug detection benefits from stronger reasoning |
| Compliance (code-reviewer, comment-analyzer, type-design-analyzer) | **sonnet** — quoting rules from CLAUDE.md is sonnet-class work |
| Custom user agents | Default sonnet; allow per-agent override in AGENT-REVIEWERS.md |

Implementation: ydy's spawn template reads the flagger's category from `discover-agents.sh` output (or a category map in SKILL.md), then picks validator model accordingly. Same shape as the existing per-agent model selection in the agent-spawn template.

When 3m6 Phase C (cross-family validator) lands, this tiering generalizes:
- Default (same family): opus/sonnet by category (above)
- Cross-family opt-in: Gemini 2.5 Pro / GPT-4o — already strongest in alternative family; category-tiering is moot


---

## DESIGN (locked 2026-05-15)

This section is authoritative for implementation.

### When the validator runs

After all agent reviewers return findings (end of round COLLECT phase, before posting). Per-finding, in parallel.

### Validator Task input

The validator receives:
- The flagger's finding text (severity, location, issue)
- The relevant PR diff context (the touched file or files referenced)
- The flagger's category indirectly via the model selection (see below)

Validator does NOT receive:
- The flagging agent's name
- The flagger's confidence rating
- Any meta-context that would let the validator rationalize the finding

### Validator output

Structured: VALID / INVALID / UNCERTAIN, with a one-line reason.

```
VERDICT: VALID
REASON: line 42 calls .toString() on response.data which is typed as `string | undefined` — the unhandled-undefined claim checks out.
```

### Filter behavior

- **VALID** → post the finding via the normal flow (post-line-comment.sh / pending-review batching)
- **INVALID** → drop silently from the round's posted findings; record in telemetry
- **UNCERTAIN** → post with a `[validator: uncertain]` annotation so the human reader sees the validator hedged

### Model selection — the simplification

**Validator model mirrors the flagger's model.** No per-category map needed; the model itself encodes the tier:

| Flagger model | Validator model |
|---------------|-----------------|
| opus (bug-class agents: silent-failure-hunter, pr-test-analyzer, code-simplifier, security-reviewer when shipped via 9ao, performance-reviewer when shipped via 9ao) | opus |
| sonnet (compliance agents: code-reviewer, comment-analyzer, type-design-analyzer) | sonnet |
| haiku or unset (cheap user agents, or no model declared) | sonnet — minimum bar for validation; haiku is too weak |
| Custom user agents with explicit model | mirror that model |

Effect: half the validators use sonnet instead of opus. Direct addresses the "not always using opus for reviewers" concern.

When **3m6 Phase C** lands later, the cross-family validator option overrides this rule (opt-in: validator becomes Gemini regardless of flagger model).

### Configuration

In AGENT-REVIEWERS.md `# Configuration` section:

```json
{
  "independent_validator": {
    "enabled": true,
    "skip_for": ["code-simplifier"],
    "uncertain_action": "post_with_annotation"
  }
}
```

Fields:
- `enabled` (default true): toggle the entire validation step
- `skip_for` (default []): list of flagger names whose findings bypass validation (useful for low-cost agents whose findings rarely benefit from validation)
- `uncertain_action` (default "post_with_annotation"): "post_with_annotation" | "post_silently" | "drop"

### Telemetry (per gx4 + spike framing)

Each finding gets a JSONL record under `.beads/pr-review-loop/findings/<pr>-<round>.jsonl`:

```json
{
  "finding_id": "round1-silent-failure-hunter-12",
  "flagger_name": "silent-failure-hunter",
  "flagger_model": "opus",
  "validator_model": "opus",
  "validator_verdict": "VALID|INVALID|UNCERTAIN",
  "validator_reason": "...",
  "posted": true,
  "posted_with_annotation": false
}
```

Aggregated at end-of-round and end-of-loop:
- Per-flagger validation acceptance rate
- Per-validator-model count (helps visualize the cost split)
- Total findings dropped by validator

### Spawning summary additions

The pre-round-1 spawning summary now includes validation state:

```
Spawning 10 reviewers (4 user + 6 defaults).
Validation: enabled (opus validators for 3 bug-class agents, sonnet validators for 7 others).
```

When validation is disabled or skipped for some agents:

```
Spawning 10 reviewers (4 user + 6 defaults).
Validation: enabled (skip_for: code-simplifier).
```

### Implementation surface

1. **`scripts/validate-finding.sh`** (new): given a finding (JSON) + flagger name + flagger model, spawn the validator Task and return verdict JSON. Helper invoked by the main loop after agents return.
2. **SKILL.md additions**: validation step in The Loop (between agent return and comment posting); validator Task prompt template; configuration spec; spawning summary update; merge-readiness summary update.
3. **`_parse_configuration.sh` extension**: parse the new `independent_validator` block; validate `uncertain_action` enum; emit usable JSON for the loop.
4. **`discover-agents.sh` extension**: surface `independent_validator` config in the configuration block of its output, so the loop can read it once at startup.
5. **Tests**: extend `tests/test-discover-agents.sh` with new scenarios for the validator config (enabled/disabled, skip_for honoured, uncertain_action enum validation). New `tests/test-validate-finding.sh` exercising the helper script with synthetic findings.

### Acceptance

- `validate-finding.sh` exists, takes (finding-json, flagger-name, flagger-model), spawns a Task with the appropriate model, returns structured verdict
- The loop's "after agents return" step calls validate-finding.sh per finding (parallel via Task tool batching, NOT shell parallelism)
- VALID findings flow to the existing posting path; INVALID dropped; UNCERTAIN posted with annotation
- Per-finding telemetry written to JSONL per gx4
- `# Configuration` accepts `independent_validator` block; defaults sensibly
- Spawning summary surfaces validation state
- Test harness extended; all assertions passing
- SKILL.md documents the validation step

### Out of scope (separate tickets)

- Cross-family validator (different model FAMILY): devon-claude-skills-3m6 Phase C
- Aggregate evaluation of validator effectiveness over a corpus: devon-claude-skills-bl7 (replay harness) + devon-claude-skills-gx4 (telemetry framework)
close_reason: Merged to main as squash commit 0d04308 via PR #21 on 2026-05-15. First cut shipped: independent validator pipeline with model-mirror rule (validator uses same model as flagger — opus for bug-class, sonnet for compliance). # Configuration.independent_validator block with strict parser validation. SKILL.md updated with validator Task prompt template, configuration spec, telemetry shape. 111 test assertions. Dogfood found 1 real null-bypass bug (// empty swallowing null/false) caught convergently by code-reviewer + Gemini — fixed with select(type=='object' and has(...)) pattern. 0np effectively addressed for validator-portion (per-default model selection already shipped via bfd; pre-flight haiku still pending v7h).

title: Epic: pr-review-loop ← aidankinzett/claude-git-pr-skill learnings
description:
Bring useful patterns from aidankinzett/claude-git-pr-skill (https://github.com/aidankinzett/claude-git-pr-skill) into the pr-review-loop and the repo overall.

## Read of the upstream

The reference skill solves a different problem from ours — it's a human-driven workflow for posting ONE batched review on someone else's PR, with an explicit pre-post approval gate. Our pr-review-loop is autonomous iteration on your own PR addressing bot/agent feedback. So the high-level shape doesn't transfer.

But three tactical patterns do:

1. **GitHub pending-review API for batched output** — they POST to /pulls/<PR>/reviews with comments[][...] attached, then submit with an event type (COMMENT/REQUEST_CHANGES/APPROVE). Our post-line-comment.sh hits /pulls/<PR>/comments one finding at a time, generating N notifications. Batching collapses these to 1 per agent run AND gives each agent a proper review state on the PR.

2. **GitHub suggestion blocks** — fenced ```suggestion blocks render as one-click 'Commit suggestion' in GitHub. Useful when agents post to a human-authored PR or when fixes are deferred.

3. **CHANGELOG.md + SemVer discipline** — they keep a clean Keep-a-Changelog file with Unreleased at the top. We have none; plugin changes are invisible to consumers.

## What we explicitly skip

- Pre-post user approval gate — anti-pattern for an autonomous loop
- gh CLI hard-stop prerequisite check — already documented in our SKILL.md
- Repo-root marketplace.json — we already have one

## Children

- devon-claude-skills-77j (P2): batch agent reviewer findings into one pending review per agent
- devon-claude-skills-08d (P3): emit GitHub suggestion blocks in agent reviewer findings (best landed with 77j)
- devon-claude-skills-ou3 (P3): add CHANGELOG.md and SemVer discipline

## Suggested order

1. **77j** — biggest UX win, foundation for 08d
2. **08d** — small extension once 77j's batched-review script exists
3. **ou3** — independent; can land anytime

title: pr-review-loop: batch agent reviewer findings into a single pending review per agent
description:
Change agent reviewer output from N individual line comments (current behavior) to a single pending GitHub review per agent run, with all findings batched and an explicit event type.

## Current behavior

scripts/post-line-comment.sh hits POST repos/.../pulls/<PR>/comments once per finding. If security-reviewer finds 8 issues, the PR author receives 8 notifications. There's no GitHub-level 'review' object, no review state, no event signal — just a pile of line comments.

## Reference pattern

aidankinzett/claude-git-pr-skill uses the pending-review pattern:

1. POST repos/.../pulls/<PR>/reviews with comments[][...] array attached → returns REVIEW_ID, state=PENDING
2. POST repos/.../pulls/<PR>/reviews/<REVIEW_ID>/events with event=COMMENT|REQUEST_CHANGES|APPROVE and an overall body

The full skill (github-pr-review SKILL.md) has the exact gh api invocations and gotchas (-f vs -F, single quotes around 'comments[][path]', RIGHT vs LEFT side).

## What to build

1. **New script** scripts/post-agent-review.sh that takes:
   - PR number
   - Agent name
   - JSON array of findings (path, line, body, optional start_line for multi-line)
   - Overall body (agent summary)
   - Event type (default COMMENT; REQUEST_CHANGES when any finding has severity >= critical; APPROVE when zero findings)

   It creates the pending review, attaches all comments, submits with the event type. Each comment body still carries the existing agent signature ('<!-- Agent: <name> -->').

2. **Update spawn prompt template** in SKILL.md (Spawning Agent Reviewers section, around L674) so agents collect findings in memory and call post-agent-review.sh ONCE at the end, instead of post-line-comment.sh per finding.

3. **Keep post-line-comment.sh** for edge cases / backward compat, but mark it deprecated in favor of the batched script.

## Event-type policy

- All findings <= important → event=COMMENT
- Any finding tagged critical → event=REQUEST_CHANGES
- Zero findings AND prior comments all resolved → event=APPROVE (rare; lets agents 'sign off')

## Why this matters

- Single notification per agent run instead of N
- The PR's GitHub 'review status' panel reflects what each agent thought
- REQUEST_CHANGES from a security-reviewer blocks merge in repos with branch protection requiring zero unresolved review-state requests — a real signal
- Aligns with the convention every other reviewer (Gemini, Cursor, human) uses

## Make event-type policy configurable (from presmihaylov review)

The original ticket proposed a fixed policy: COMMENT for normal, REQUEST_CHANGES on critical, APPROVE when clean. But peer skills disagree on the right default:

- **presmihaylov/claude github-code-reviewer** — strict COMMENT-only; "humans make approval decisions"
- **anthropics/claude-code code-review** (Boris) — severity-driven (REQUEST_CHANGES on critical)
- **aidankinzett/claude-git-pr-skill** — single-event-on-finding, user-approved

Treat the event-policy choice as a spike (per bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`). Make it configurable with named presets:

```yaml
review_event_policy:
  preset: severity_based   # or: strict_comment_only | always_approve_when_clean
  # or fully custom:
  custom:
    on_critical_finding: REQUEST_CHANGES   # or COMMENT
    on_clean: APPROVE                       # or COMMENT
    default: COMMENT
```

Default preset: `severity_based` (matches our existing planned behavior), but users can switch to `strict_comment_only` if they want their loop's reviews to always be advisory.

Telemetry (gx4) should capture which preset was used per round so we can later analyze whether REQUEST_CHANGES events actually surface real-world blockers vs. just creating churn.

## Reference implementation: presmihaylov's submit_review.py

presmihaylov ships a clean Python implementation of exactly the pending-review pattern this ticket proposes: https://github.com/presmihaylov/claude/blob/master/.claude/skills/github-code-reviewer/scripts/submit_review.py

Key choices worth following:

1. **Comments-file JSON pattern** — write the findings array to /tmp/pr-<N>-review.json, then submit with --comments-file. Avoids fragile multi-arg gh-api invocation; debuggable; serializable for telemetry/replay (gx4, bl7).
2. **Side: RIGHT default** — all findings live on the added/modified lines.
3. **start_line + start_side: RIGHT** for multi-line comments.
4. **Commit SHA fetched via `gh pr view --json headRefOid`** if not provided.
5. **POST to repos/<owner>/<repo>/pulls/<PR>/reviews with payload `{commit_id, event, comments[], body?}`** — single request, atomic.

Use this script as the starting point for scripts/post-agent-review.sh (the new batched-review script this ticket creates), translating from Python to bash where simpler, or keeping as Python if cleaner.

## HEREDOC review-body templates (from comprehensive-pr-reviewer review)

udecode's reviewing-pr skill (see bd memory `provenance-comprehensive-pr-reviewer`) uses two HEREDOC-templated formats for the batched review body — one for incremental rounds, one for full-diff rounds. The shapes are similar but the incremental version explicitly opens with "since the last commit/round, I see:" to signal scope.

When implementing the batched-review script (post-agent-review.sh), use two body templates parameterized by round-scope:

**Full-diff template (round 1 or `--full` override):**

```
## Review by <agent-name>

<findings, one per line, category-prefixed>

---

<optional: summary, deferred items, beads tickets created>
```

**Incremental template (rounds 2+ in incremental mode, per devon-claude-skills-3z8):**

```
## Round <N> review by <agent-name>

Reviewing commits <prior-bookmark>..HEAD (<commit count> commits since round <N-1>).

<findings, one per line, category-prefixed>

---

<optional: which prior-round findings were addressed, which remain>
```

Render templates with simple bash heredoc / Python f-strings depending on what scripting we land on for post-agent-review.sh (presmihaylov's submit_review.py is in Python; matching makes maintenance easier).

The incremental template's reference to round number and bookmark range is what makes round-over-round tracking visible to the human reading the PR — without it, "round 2 review" looks identical to "round 1 review" except for content.

title: Epic: pr-review-loop ← upstream pr-review-toolkit learnings
description:
Bring the pr-review-loop skill up to par with anthropics/claude-plugins-official pr-review-toolkit (https://github.com/anthropics/claude-plugins-official/tree/main/plugins/pr-review-toolkit), which is essentially 'agent reviewers with sensible defaults' built on native Claude Code capabilities.

## Read of the upstream

Our pr-review-loop is process-rich (loop orchestration, multi-bot Gemini/Cursor/Claude, CI fix loop, stopping heuristics, self-contradiction detection, beads integration) but specialist-poor: agent reviewers are bring-your-own via AGENT-REVIEWERS.md.

The upstream pr-review-toolkit is the inverse: process-light, but ships:
- 6 well-scoped specialist reviewer agents with confidence scoring and structured output
- Native Claude Code subagent integration (frontmatter name/description/model/color → /agents list, typed Task subagent_type, colored named chips in the Task panel)
- A slash command with argument-hint and allowed-tools
- A plugin.json manifest

Combined, the two are complementary. This epic adopts the parts of the upstream that strengthen our skill without giving up our orchestration advantages.

## Children

- devon-claude-skills-bfd (P2): ship 6 default specialist reviewer agents
- devon-claude-skills-oma (P2): adopt Claude Code native subagent + plugin manifest capabilities
- devon-claude-skills-q2h (P2): 'audit AGENT-REVIEWERS.md against defaults' subcommand (blocked on bfd)
- devon-claude-skills-gzk (P3): optional code-simplifier polish round before merge (blocked on bfd)
- devon-claude-skills-rb7 (P3): confidence threshold (>=80/100) in agent spawn prompt
- devon-claude-skills-zbn (P3): structured Critical/Important/Suggestions output in summaries
- devon-claude-skills-745 (P3): concrete 'When to invoke' scenarios in agent-spawn prompt

## Suggested order

1. **oma** (native subagent + plugin.json) — foundation; shipping defaults as native subagents in step 2 depends on this
2. **bfd** (ship defaults) — biggest UX win
3. **q2h** (audit existing AGENT-REVIEWERS.md against defaults) — gives existing-customization projects a path to adopt defaults
4. **rb7 / 745** (spawn-prompt tweaks) — small, can land in parallel
5. **gzk** (code-simplifier polish round) — once code-simplifier is shipped via bfd
6. **zbn** (structured output) — can land anytime

title: pr-review-loop: adopt Claude Code native subagent + plugin manifest capabilities
description:
Migrate from custom AGENT-REVIEWERS.md parsing + generic 'general-purpose' Task spawning to Claude Code's native subagent system, and fill out the plugin manifest.

## What upstream uses that we don't

Upstream pr-review-toolkit (https://github.com/anthropics/claude-plugins-official/tree/main/plugins/pr-review-toolkit) declares each reviewer as a native Claude Code subagent under agents/*.md with frontmatter:

```
---
name: code-reviewer
description: Use this agent when ...
model: opus           # or 'inherit'
color: green
---
```

This gives them, for free:
- Registration in Claude Code's /agents list
- Task tool subagent_type: code-reviewer (typed, not generic)
- Colored named chips in the Task panel UI when running
- Per-agent model selection
- Discoverability and self-documenting trigger via the description

They also declare their command with:
```
---
description: 'Comprehensive PR review using specialized agents'
argument-hint: '[review-aspects]'
allowed-tools: ['Bash', 'Glob', 'Grep', 'Read', 'Task']
---
```

And ship a .claude-plugin/plugin.json with name/description/author.

## Where we are

- plugins/pr-review-loop/.claude-plugin/ exists but is EMPTY — no plugin.json
- Our agent-reviewer system is custom: discover-agents.sh parses AGENT-REVIEWERS.md, then SKILL.md instructs spawning Task with subagent_type=general-purpose and prompt-injected instructions
- The user-visible UI for every reviewer is identical 'general-purpose' chip — no name, no color, no model variation

## What to do

1. **plugin.json** — add a manifest to plugins/pr-review-loop/.claude-plugin/plugin.json (name, description, author). Required for marketplace listing anyway.

2. **Native subagent files** — when we ship the 6 defaults (devon-claude-skills-bfd), put them as plugins/pr-review-loop/agents/*.md with proper Claude Code frontmatter (name/description/model/color), NOT as a starter AGENT-REVIEWERS.md template. Users then get them registered automatically.

3. **Hybrid spawn path** — extend SKILL.md so when an agent reviewer is also registered as a native subagent (by name match), spawn it via subagent_type=<name> instead of subagent_type=general-purpose. Falls back to general-purpose for purely user-authored AGENT-REVIEWERS.md entries. This is the key UX win — review rounds show distinct colored named chips.

4. **Per-agent model selection** — let AGENT-REVIEWERS.md entries declare a model (or inherit it from the native subagent), and pass model=... when spawning Tasks. Today we hardcode model:sonnet in the spawn prompt template.

5. **Slash command** — consider adding commands/pr-review.md with argument-hint and allowed-tools so users can launch the loop via /pr-review-loop:pr-review. Today the skill is only triggerable via natural language.

## Why this matters

The user observation that the upstream is 'doing some special stuff in the claude code display' is exactly this — they're consuming first-class Claude Code plugin capabilities (subagents, commands, manifest) that we haven't picked up. Adopting them gives us better UX (named/colored review tasks) and proper marketplace integration without much code.

title: pr-review-loop: 'audit AGENT-REVIEWERS.md against defaults' subcommand
description:
When a project already has an AGENT-REVIEWERS.md, the skill should be able to diff it against the 6 default specialist reviewers (see sibling ticket) and recommend additions, replacements, or improvements.

## Why

After we ship default specialists, projects with an existing AGENT-REVIEWERS.md will not automatically benefit. We need a way to say 'here is what you have, here is what the defaults provide, here is what would strengthen your setup' — without overwriting user customizations.

## What it should do

Given a repo with AGENT-REVIEWERS.md:

1. Parse the user's existing agents (H2 headings under '# Agents')
2. Compare against the 6 default specialist agents by name and focus area
3. Output a structured recommendation:
   - **Missing**: defaults the user does not have at all (e.g. no silent-failure-hunter)
   - **Overlap**: user has a same-name or near-duplicate agent — show both definitions side-by-side and let the user decide whether to replace, merge, or keep
   - **Coverage gaps**: focus areas (error handling, test coverage, types, comments, simplification) that no user agent covers
   - **Better-scoped variants**: cases where the default has tighter focus or quantitative scoring (e.g. confidence ≥80) that the user agent lacks
4. Never modify AGENT-REVIEWERS.md without user approval; emit a proposed diff/patch

## How invoked

Could be a separate skill entry point, a subcommand of the loop, or a one-shot script under scripts/. TBD — design before implementing.

## Acceptance

- Runs against a repo with AGENT-REVIEWERS.md and produces a clear additions/changes recommendation
- Runs against a repo without AGENT-REVIEWERS.md and offers to scaffold one with the 6 defaults (or whichever subset is relevant given the languages/frameworks in the repo)
- Respects user customizations; never silently overwrites

## Also check for philosophy conflicts

When auditing a user's existing AGENT-REVIEWERS.md, additionally scan each user-authored agent's instructions for rules that conflict with our touch-it-you-own-it philosophy (see devon-claude-skills-rb7 and bd memory `code-review-philosophy-when-you-touch-code-you`).

Specifically flag agents that say things like:
- "Skip pre-existing issues"
- "Only flag bugs introduced in this PR"
- "Don't review general quality concerns"
- Anything that narrows scope by *origin* (when the bug was introduced) rather than by *touched-file boundary*

For each such conflict, surface it under a new bucket in the audit output:

- **Philosophy conflict**: rule X in agent Y contradicts the touch-it-you-own-it stance — recommend rewording so the agent flags issues in touched files regardless of pre-existence, with triage routed through the existing out-of-scope/beads flow.

title: pr-review-loop: ship 6 default specialist reviewer agents
description:
Adopt the 6 specialist agents from anthropics/claude-plugins-official pr-review-toolkit as default agent reviewers, so users get useful reviews without authoring AGENT-REVIEWERS.md.

## Background

Our pr-review-loop is process-heavy (loop orchestration, multi-bot, CI handling) but specialist-light: agent reviewers are bring-your-own via AGENT-REVIEWERS.md. The upstream pr-review-toolkit (https://github.com/anthropics/claude-plugins-official/tree/main/plugins/pr-review-toolkit) ships 6 well-scoped reviewer agents:

- code-reviewer (general CLAUDE.md compliance, confidence-scored 0-100)
- silent-failure-hunter (empty catches, optional-chain swallowing, fallback masking)
- pr-test-analyzer (behavioral coverage gaps, 1-10 criticality)
- comment-analyzer (comment rot, factual accuracy vs code)
- type-design-analyzer (4-axis 1-10 ratings on invariants/encapsulation)
- code-simplifier (post-review polish — see also separate ticket for final-polish round)

## What to do

1. Add these 6 agent definitions to the plugin (likely under plugins/pr-review-loop/agents/ or as a starter AGENT-REVIEWERS.md template)
2. Update the discovery/spawn flow so they're used as defaults when no AGENT-REVIEWERS.md exists
3. Allow user-authored AGENT-REVIEWERS.md to override/disable defaults by name
4. Document the defaults in SKILL.md

## Notes

- Upstream source: gh api repos/anthropics/claude-plugins-official/contents/plugins/pr-review-toolkit/agents/<name>.md
- License is MIT; attribute appropriately
- Related ticket needed: 'recommend additions/changes against defaults' for projects that already have an AGENT-REVIEWERS.md (see follow-up)

## Conflict with upstream filter rules — DO NOT inherit

The upstream pr-review-toolkit ships these 6 agents alongside a `/review-pr` command that applies filter rules contradicting our touch-it-you-own-it philosophy (see devon-claude-skills-rb7 and the persisted bd memory `code-review-philosophy-when-you-touch-code-you`).

Specifically, the upstream filters out:
- Pre-existing issues
- General quality concerns (e.g. test coverage) unless explicitly in CLAUDE.md

We treat both as IN SCOPE for touched files (triage trivial → fix, non-trivial → beads).

When shipping the 6 agents:

1. **Ship the agent bodies as-is** — they don't contain those filter rules themselves; the rules live in the upstream command. The agent bodies are clean.
2. **Audit each agent's body for any implicit "introduced in this PR" language** before shipping. Strip anything that narrows scope to introduced-only.
3. **Do NOT port the upstream `/review-pr` command's filter list.** Our agent-spawn prompt (per devon-claude-skills-rb7) supplies the correct rules.
4. **pr-test-analyzer is naturally aligned** with our philosophy — its whole purpose is the "test coverage" category Boris's command says to skip. Ship it without apology.
5. **silent-failure-hunter is naturally aligned** — error-handling gaps in touched code are exactly the kind of thing we want flagged regardless of whether the bug pre-existed.
6. **code-reviewer needs a closer read** — Boris's confidence ≥80 rubric is fine to keep, but if the body anywhere implies "only flag issues introduced in this PR," replace with touch-it-you-own-it language from rb7.

## Per-specialist checklist content (sontek source material)

When authoring each native subagent's body, use the relevant slice of sontek's comprehensive checklist (https://mcpmarket.com/tools/skills/code-review-expert-8) as scoped guidance for what to look for. Mapping:

- **silent-failure-hunter** → Correctness & Logic (runtime errors, edge cases, error handling) + Side Effects & Impact (monitoring/logging for critical operations)
- **pr-test-analyzer** → Testing (coverage requirements, test quality, how-to-verify section)
- **type-design-analyzer** → Design & Architecture (consistency, SOLID, API design, error boundaries) + Correctness (type safety)
- **comment-analyzer** → Code Quality (readability, documentation, dead code, magic numbers)
- **code-reviewer** (CLAUDE.md compliance) → use CLAUDE.md content directly; sontek's checklist is for the OTHER specialists
- **code-simplifier** → Code Quality (complexity, readability) + Design & Architecture (DRY without over-abstracting)

For security-reviewer and performance-reviewer (devon-claude-skills-9ao), see that ticket for their checklists.

## What NOT to copy from sontek

- His skill bundles approval/reject decision-making ("Approve when..." section). Our loop doesn't decide approval; we iterate until convergence and let CI/branch protection gate merge. Skip this section.
- His "Long-Term Considerations" list is better handled by the sensitive-area escalation gate (devon-claude-skills-vsf), not buried in agent bodies.
