## DESIGN (locked 2026-05-14)

This section is authoritative for implementation. Earlier sections above describe motivation and source material.

### What ships

Six native subagent definitions under `plugins/pr-review-loop/agents/` (per devon-claude-skills-oma's native-subagent integration):

1. **code-reviewer** — CLAUDE.md compliance, confidence-scored
2. **silent-failure-hunter** — empty catches, broad excepts, optional-chain swallowing, fallback masking
3. **pr-test-analyzer** — behavioral test coverage gaps, 1-10 criticality
4. **comment-analyzer** — comment rot, factual accuracy vs code
5. **type-design-analyzer** — encapsulation, invariant expression, usefulness, enforcement (4 axes 1-10)
6. **code-simplifier** — post-review polish (only flag if commit fully fixes)

Each definition follows our `focus / what to check / flag issues if / do NOT flag / when flagging suggest` template, with touch-it-you-own-it scope rules (rb7) baked in.

### Override semantics — "C+E" (always-on defaults, additive user agents, same-name override, per-default opt-out)

| User repo state | What spawns |
|-----------------|-------------|
| No `AGENT-REVIEWERS.md` | All 6 defaults |
| Has `AGENT-REVIEWERS.md` with no `# Agents` section | All 6 defaults + user `# Guidelines` / `# Context` apply |
| Has `AGENT-REVIEWERS.md` with custom agents under `# Agents` | All 6 defaults + user custom agents (additive) |
| Has user agent with same name as a default (e.g., `## code-reviewer`) | User's version replaces the default; other 5 defaults still spawn |
| Has `# Configuration` with `disabled: [pr-test-analyzer]` | 5 defaults spawn (no pr-test-analyzer) |
| Hierarchical scoping (sub-AGENT-REVIEWERS.md) | Subdirectory file can override / disable defaults JUST for that subtree, matching existing depth-priority semantics |

### `# Configuration` section spec

New H1 section in AGENT-REVIEWERS.md. All fields optional; section itself is optional.

```markdown
# Configuration

defaults_version_checked: 1.3.0

disabled:
  - pr-test-analyzer

overlap_acknowledged:
  - my_pci_auditor:
      overlaps_with: security-reviewer
      reason: "PCI-compliance-specific scope; want both running because the default is general security"
```

Field semantics:

- **`defaults_version_checked: <pr-review-loop plugin version>`** — the plugin version whose defaults the user has reviewed. Bumped automatically by the `/pr-review-loop:audit-agents` tool when the user accepts/rejects each recommendation. Same version namespace as the plugin itself — no separate "defaults version."
- **`disabled:`** — list of default agent names the user does NOT want spawned. Per-name opt-out.
- **`overlap_acknowledged:`** — map. Key is the user's agent name; value has `overlaps_with` (the default name) and `reason` (free text). **`reason` is REQUIRED** — parser rejects entries without it. Purpose: future readers of AGENT-REVIEWERS.md see why both agents are intentionally running, not perceive it as cleanup-eligible cruft.

### Stale-pin detection at loop start

Before round 1, the loop:

1. Reads `defaults_version_checked` from `# Configuration` (defaults to `0.0.0` if missing or no `# Configuration` section).
2. Compares to the installed plugin version.
3. **If mismatch (or `0.0.0`):**
   - Print a structured message naming the old version vs current, the count of defaults that changed, and the exact command to run:
     ```
     ⚠️  AGENT-REVIEWERS.md was last audited against pr-review-loop v1.2.5.
        Installed version: v1.3.0
        Run `/pr-review-loop:audit-agents` to review changes, or
        pass `--skip-stale-check` to proceed with current configuration.
     ```
   - **Exit non-zero (pause).** User must either run the audit (q2h) or re-run with `--skip-stale-check`.
4. **If match:** silent proceed; no inference, no auto-overlap-check at startup.

The startup check is purely a version-pin comparison — deterministic, no LLM judgment. This is critical: stochastic inference at startup would fire differently across runs, eroding user trust.

### `--skip-stale-check` override

For experienced users who know their config is fine and don't want to re-audit:

- Loop accepts the flag; bypasses the stale check; proceeds normally.
- Loop's **merge-readiness summary at end** includes a one-liner noting the bypass:
  ```
  Note: ran with stale defaults pin (config v1.2.5, plugin v1.3.0). Consider running `/pr-review-loop:audit-agents`.
  ```
- This ensures a bypass is never silently forgotten.

### Lightweight overlap detection lives in the audit tool, NOT in bfd

bfd ships the deterministic infrastructure (defaults + override semantics + stale-pin + warning + --skip-stale-check).

Deeper overlap analysis — keyword-overlap pre-filter, LLM-judgment overlap rating, side-by-side diff, recommend-rename outcome — lives in **q2h (audit AGENT-REVIEWERS.md against defaults)**. q2h runs only when the user explicitly invokes `/pr-review-loop:audit-agents`. q2h's behavior:

1. Scan user agents for keyword overlap with each default (cheap pre-filter)
2. For candidates, LLM-rate overlap 1-10
3. Surface recommendations:
   - **Rename**: "Your `security-expert` strongly overlaps with default `security-reviewer`. Rename to `security-reviewer` to engage same-name override."
   - **Acknowledge**: "Confirm you want both — adds an `overlap_acknowledged` entry with required reason."
   - **Disable**: "Disable the default — adds to `disabled` list."
4. Apply accepted recommendations to AGENT-REVIEWERS.md
5. Bump `defaults_version_checked` to current plugin version

### Visibility at loop start

When the loop proceeds (either stale check passed, or `--skip-stale-check` used), emit a one-line summary of what's spawning so the user can verify configuration:

```
Spawning 6 reviewers: code-reviewer (5 defaults + 1 user override: code-reviewer overrides default)
```

Or for an additive scenario:

```
Spawning 7 reviewers: 6 defaults + 1 custom (security-expert from AGENT-REVIEWERS.md)
```

Goes to the same place we report other setup state (TodoWrite block or pre-round summary).

### Polish surface (this ticket's scope)

1. Six default subagent files (markdown with frontmatter per oma's native-subagent format)
2. `discover-agents.sh` extended to merge defaults with user agents per C+E semantics
3. `# Configuration` section parser (extends existing AGENT-REVIEWERS.md parsing in `discover-agents.sh`)
4. Stale-pin detection in the loop's pre-round-1 setup
5. `--skip-stale-check` flag plumbed through loop entry
6. Pre-round-1 spawning-summary emission
7. Merge-readiness summary updated for bypass note
8. SKILL.md documents:
   - All 6 defaults (one-line description each)
   - C+E override semantics with worked examples 1-5 from the table above
   - `# Configuration` section schema
   - Loop behavior on stale pin
   - How to use `--skip-stale-check`

### Out of scope (separate tickets)

- Deep audit / overlap detection — q2h
- Adding security-reviewer + performance-reviewer specialists (8 total) — 9ao (blocks on bfd)
- Native-subagent migration if not yet done — oma (bfd uses oma's mechanism; check dependency order)

### Acceptance

- All 6 defaults exist as native subagents, scoped per oma's mechanism
- C+E semantics implemented in `discover-agents.sh`
- `# Configuration` section parser accepts the spec above; rejects `overlap_acknowledged` entries without `reason`
- Loop exits non-zero on stale pin without `--skip-stale-check`
- Loop emits clear instruction to run `/pr-review-loop:audit-agents` on stale pin
- Loop emits spawning summary at round-1 start
- Loop emits stale-bypass note in merge-readiness summary when `--skip-stale-check` used
- SKILL.md updated with all of the above
- Test: a clean repo with no AGENT-REVIEWERS.md spawns all 6 defaults
- Test: a repo with one user agent matching a default name overrides correctly
- Test: a repo with `# Configuration` disabled list excludes the named default
- Test: a repo with stale `defaults_version_checked` and no `--skip-stale-check` exits non-zero

title: yt-dlp progress output pollutes stdout JSON in screenshotter scripts
description:
On a first-time download, yt-dlp prints download progress to stdout. The youtube-screenshotter scripts (discover.py, extract.py, video.py) emit their JSON manifests to stdout too, so the JSON is corrupted with progress lines on the very first run for any new video. Subsequent calls work because the video is cached.

Workaround in use: re-run discover.py after the first call triggered the download. The 15-video batch handled this in every Task, ~6 of 15 needed an explicit re-run.

Real fix: tell yt-dlp to suppress progress output (--no-progress, or --quiet, or redirect its progress to stderr) when called from inside these scripts. Verify the metadata-print path also stays on stderr.

Acceptance: a fresh first-time download via discover.py produces clean JSON on stdout that python json.load() parses without error.

title: Phase 1.8: A.6 pHash dedup over-collapses channels with template-style frames
description:
The cross-time pHash dedup in Phase A.6 over-collapses conceptually distinct frames when a channel uses a recurring dual-frame template (e.g. illustration on one side + presenter inset on the other). The template pixels dominate the global pHash, so two frames with completely different illustrations register as near-duplicates and the later one gets dropped.

Observed across the entire 15-video Grainbound batch (2026-04-29): every video had A.6 wrongly merge 3-15 distinct conceptual frames into earlier template-shaped keepers. Phase A.7's vision-based audit had to restore them in every case. The audit catches the bug, but it's bug-prone and burns sub-agent budget.

Possible fixes:
1. Mask the presenter-inset region before computing pHash. Requires per-channel layout config or auto-detection of the recurring inset region.
2. Move pHash dedup to AFTER A.7 audit, so the audit dedupes by content rather than by template-similarity. The audit already reads frames with vision; have it return groups instead of just keep/drop decisions.
3. Replace pHash with vision-based dedup entirely (read both images, ask 'are these the same scene?'). More expensive but template-blind.
4. Auto-detect template patterns: if many candidate dedup pairs share the same large pixel region, mask that region and re-pHash.

Acceptance: re-run a Grainbound video. A.7 audit's keep-list before/after A.6 dedup should be near-identical (no template-collapse to undo).

title: Phase 1.7: motion-bisection for animated sequences
description:
The Phase 1.6 e2e against MIqpvpNS5pI revealed a gap: per-second pHash run-grouping merges sequences where only a SMALL OBJECT moves across an otherwise-static background (e.g. a boat moving across a long landscape with river+hills+sky). The global pHash stays within threshold because most pixels don't change, so the run-grouping treats it as one stable shot and emits only the start frame.

Concrete miss: t=358.792 captured the boat at the LEFT edge of a wide river scene, but the boat's animation across the river over the next ~20 seconds was lost. The user's note: 'we should bisect more when we see a lot of motion happening.'

Implementation options (pick one or combine):

1. Post-classification motion bisection: after a frame is classified as a keep-kind, automatically extract t±2, t±5, t±10 (or until next keep-kind) and look for distinctly-different keep-kind frames in those samples. Add survivors to the keep-list. Cheapest option, fits cleanly in synthesizer Phase A.5.
2. Grid-pHash in screenshotter: split each thumbnail into a 4x4 grid and compute a pHash per cell. A small object moving across a static background changes one or two cells without affecting the global pHash. Run-grouping then merges only when ALL cells are similar to the run's start. More compute, better motion sensitivity.
3. ffmpeg motion-vector primitive: use mestimate or a lower-threshold scene-detect (~0.05) to flag motion-rich regions and emit additional candidates within them.

Acceptance: re-run against MIqpvpNS5pI; the boat-on-river section captures multiple boat positions instead of one edge frame.
notes:
External design input (from a separate Claude conversation, 2026-04-29):

DETECTION SIGNAL — sustained directional optical flow distinguishes progressive animation cleanly:
- Static frame: zero flow
- Static reveal (text/diagram appears in place): zero flow, pHash drifts
- Progressive animation: sustained flow vectors with consistent direction across many frames
This is the third case our current primitives can't catch. Once a span is flagged, dense-resample (0.1-0.25s interval) across the run boundaries we already have from pHash grouping — cost is nothing.

SELECTION WITHIN WINDOW — pick the frame where the moving subject is fully visible and centered, computable from the Farneback flow field with no model:

```python
mag = np.linalg.norm(flow, axis=2)
mask = mag > flow_threshold  # binary: moving pixels

# largest connected component = the subject
nlabels, labels, stats, centroids = cv2.connectedComponentsWithStats(mask.astype(np.uint8))
if nlabels < 2:
    score = 0
else:
    largest = 1 + np.argmax(stats[1:, cv2.CC_STAT_AREA])
    cx, cy = centroids[largest]
    x, y, w, h, area = stats[largest]
    h_frame, w_frame = mask.shape
    touches_edge = x == 0 or y == 0 or x+w >= w_frame or y+h >= h_frame
    center_dist = np.hypot(cx - w_frame/2, cy - h_frame/2) / np.hypot(w_frame/2, h_frame/2)
    score = area * (1 - center_dist) * (0.2 if touches_edge else 1.0)
```

Pick frame in dense resample with max score. Boat-at-edge scores low (touches boundary), boat-in-center scores high (interior + centered).

REFINEMENTS:
1. Connected-components assumes one moving subject — breaks for "lord on horse riding past peasants tending crops." Score combined motion-mask bbox or sum top-K.
2. Binary edge-touch penalty is crude — a close-up cart filling 80% of frame always touches edges and gets unfairly docked. Soften: penalize proportional to fraction of component bbox perimeter coinciding with frame perimeter.

THE BIGGER POINT — `--allow-no-screenshot` mode:
For some progressive animations the prose carries the meaning ("goods moved by water") and the visual is decoration. If max score in a motion cluster falls below threshold, emit a markdown annotation instead of a screenshot:
> [animation: cart and boat moving across landscape, 0:34-0:48]

This produces better notes than always finding some frame to grab. Fits the value-filter principle already in Phase A.

This unifies options 1+3 from the existing description with a deterministic selection rule and an opt-out. Recommend implementing as: (a) optical-flow primitive in screenshotter, (b) synthesizer Phase A motion-bisection step that calls it, (c) Phase D composer learns the markdown-annotation fallback.

title: synthesizer SKILL.md Phase A: rewrite to consume discover.py output
description:
Replace synthesizer Phase A's current sampling+bisection prose with a flow that consumes screenshotter discover.py.

New Phase A:
A.1 Fetch transcript and metadata (unchanged)
A.2 Call discover.py to get candidate-timestamp list. Manifest contains both phash_runs (sustained content with run_duration) and scene_detect transitions.

A.6 Cross-time pHash dedup: walk the entire keep-list (not just adjacent) and drop any frame whose pHash is within Hamming ≤ 12 of an earlier-kept frame. Catches the case where the same world-map appears at chapter 1 opening AND chapter 3 opening (image-01 vs image-13 in Phase 1.5).
A.7 Emit final keep-list of (timestamp, frame_path, kind) triples.

title: screenshotter: add discover.py with ffmpeg scene-detect + per-second pHash run-grouping (hybrid mode)
description:
New script: plugins/youtube-screenshotter/skills/youtube-screenshotter/scripts/discover.py

Purpose: emit a high-recall list of candidate-timestamp triples (timestamp, run_duration, source_signal) for a video, using ffmpeg primitives. Synthesizer Phase A consumes this and decides which to keep via kind classification.

CLI:
  discover.py <URL-or-file> [-o WORK_DIR] [--threshold-scene 0.2] [--threshold-phash 12] [--min-run 3]

Behavior:
1. Resolve <URL-or-file>: if URL, ensure video is downloaded via existing video.py. If file path, use it directly.
2. Run ffmpeg scene-detect at --threshold-scene; capture transition timestamps. Cost: single pass, ~10s for 18min video.
3. Run ffmpeg single-pass per-second thumbnail extraction at low res (-vf 'fps=1,scale=320:180' -q:v 5) into WORK_DIR/discover-thumbs/. Cost: ~30s for 18min video, ~30MB.
4. Compute pHash for each thumbnail (importable function from existing phash.py, or shell out per file).
5. Group consecutive thumbnails into runs where Hamming-distance to run's start frame is ≤ --threshold-phash. (Threshold 12 merges talking-head microexpressions into single runs; threshold 5 was too tight and produced 628 runs of 1-2s each.)
6. Filter runs to duration ≥ --min-run seconds.
7. Output JSON manifest:
   {
     'video_path': '...',
     'duration_seconds': 1119,
     'sources': {
       'scene_detect': [4.8, 6.9, ...],
       'phash_runs': [{'start_t': 82, 'end_t': 86, 'duration': 5, 'phash': '...'}, ...]
     },
     'candidates': [{'timestamp': 82.0, 'kind_hint': 'phash_run', 'run_duration': 5}, {'timestamp': 4.8, 'kind_hint': 'scene_change', 'run_duration': null}, ...]
   }
   The 'candidates' list is the union of scene-detect timestamps and phash-run starts, deduped by proximity (within 1s of an existing entry).

Acceptance criteria (re-run against MIqpvpNS5pI):
- Catches the TOLL diagram start (~t=222) — phash_runs source
- Catches Three-Day Rule diagram (~t=88) — phash_runs source
- Catches lord+castle at t=258 — scene_detect source
- Catches missed terrain map at t=642 (18s run) — phash_runs source
- Total candidates: 60-90 (vs the synthesizer's 42 sparse samples in Phase 1.5)
- All standard library + existing screenshotter scripts; no new Python deps.

Both scene-detect and phash_run sources are working, union+dedup operates as designed.

title: Phase 1.6: image discovery primitives in screenshotter
description:
The Phase 1.5 e2e (PR #12) showed that the synthesizer's sparse-sample-then-bisect approach has poor recall — missed the iconic TOLL chokepoint diagram, the surplus wheat field, multiple ADDS VALUE build-up frames, and a detailed terrain map (run #359, 18s static). The user explicitly wants 'more and more ffmpeg capability over time' added to youtube-screenshotter as the discovery layer.

Goal: replace the synthesizer's manual sampling with a screenshotter-side 'discover.py' tool that uses ffmpeg primitives to produce a high-recall candidate-timestamp list. Synthesizer Phase A becomes: 'call discover.py, re-extract candidates at full res via extract.py, classify by kind.' The screenshotter stays mechanical; the synthesizer stays smart.

Discovery primitives evaluated against MIqpvpNS5pI:
- ffmpeg scene-detect (threshold 0.2): 56 candidates, fast, but MISSES soft-fade diagram starts (Three-Day Rule, TOLL). Catches some short content stretches per-second-pHash filters out (lord+castle at t=258).
- per-second extraction + pHash run-grouping (threshold 12, run ≥3s): comprehensive, single ffmpeg pass with -vf fps=1 -s 320x180 is fast (~30s, ~30MB on disk).
- Hybrid: union of both. ~60-80 candidates per ~18min video. Best recall.
- silencedetect: useless for narration-heavy videos (no silent stretches > 2s in this one).
- freezedetect (n=0.005, d=2): catches every sustained shot including talking-head poses AND diagrams. Useful but doesn't distinguish kinds.

Candidate-timestamp scope only — kind classification stays in the synthesizer.

See bd memories: youtube-synthesizer-phase-1-5-e2e-against-miqpvpns5pi (image count baseline) and youtube-synthesizer-phash-dedup-threshold-5-hamming-is (cross-time dedup gap).

title: synthesizer Phase D: chapter-level prose flow with inline images (option D)
description:
Replace the per-scene heading hierarchy with chapter-level prose flow.

Current behavior (broken):
- ## Scenes / ### chapter / #### Scene N — MM:SS to MM:SS / image / transcript-span structure
- Transcript text per scene = naive concatenation of snippets within the scene's [start_time, end_time) window
- Result: clauses cut mid-sentence at scene boundaries; e.g. '...use the map and work / along. I will not show…'

New behavior:
- One ## section per chapter from chapter_markers
- USE THE AUTHOR'S CHAPTER TITLES VERBATIM — including placeholders like 'Chapter 2', 'Chapter 3'. The chapter structure is the author's deliberate framing. Do NOT synthesize 'better' titles from content.
- Sentence-aware reflow of the full chapter's transcript span: concatenate snippets, break into paragraphs at sentence terminators (. ! ?) at natural feeling points (~3-6 sentences)
- Embed informational images inline at their timestamps as block-level ![[image-NN.png]] elements, snapping to the nearest sentence boundary
- Multiple images in close succession (animated build-up) appear consecutively with whatever prose falls between them
- Words verbatim — soft-corrects per Phase C policy applied during reflow, logged to frontmatter

Body sections:
- ## TL;DR
- ## Key takeaways
- ## <chapter 1 title>  (verbatim from chapter_markers)
  prose with inline images
- ## <chapter 2 title>
  prose with inline images
- ...
- ## References  (filtered URLs from description)
- ## Commands & Code  (omit if empty)

Very short videos (≤ 3 min) or videos with no chapter_markers: render the entire transcript as one prose flow under '## Body' (or similar — open). No chapter sections.

See docs/youtube-wiki-design.md → 'Body structure (option D — decoupled prose flow)'.

title: synthesizer Phase C: description-driven references + hallucination check + soft-correct policy
description:
Extend Phase C beyond the existing TL;DR / takeaways / tags / topics / commands extraction with three new responsibilities.

1. Description-driven references:
   - Fetch the video description via yt-dlp (info['description'])
   - Extract URLs (regex on http/https/www. prefixes; expand bare domain references)
   - Classify each URL per the keep/drop table in the design doc:
     - KEEP: author resource pages, project/tool homepages, papers/articles/books, GitHub, related videos, community spaces with specific in-video reference, standalone documentation
     - DROP: Patreon, merch/store, sponsor codes, generic social media, generic newsletters, linktree-style aggregators
   - Emit kept references as bullets with (a) the actual URL and (b) a one-line annotation noting why it was cited
   - Also emit plain-text references for people/papers named in-video without explicit URLs

2. Hallucination check via description:
   - Compare the agent-generated TL;DR against the description's 'ABOUT THIS VIDEO' prose (or full description if no clear 'about' section)
   - If TL;DR introduces framing or claims not grounded in either the transcript or description, re-do the TL;DR
   - Bias toward attribution-implicit rather than asserting claims as fact (a sources/videos/ entry is by definition 'what the video said')

3. Auto-caption soft-correct policy:
   - When walking the transcript for prose composition, identify obvious mis-transcriptions: homophones (kills↔scales, their↔there, write↔right), garbled compound words, dropped articles in otherwise grammatical sentences
   - Soft-correct only when the intended word is unambiguous from immediate context
   - Bias toward leaving alone — better one piece of garbled text than an inverted meaning
   - Log every correction in 'transcript_corrections' frontmatter

See docs/youtube-wiki-design.md → 'Description-driven references', 'Description as hallucination check', 'Auto-caption soft-correct policy'.

title: synthesizer Phase A: value-filter sampling (replaces scene-boundary detection)
description:
Replace the same/different/additive verdict scheme in Phase A.4 with a value-based kind classification.

Current behavior (broken):
- pHash + agent vision classify each consecutive pair as same/different/additive
- 'different' marks a scene boundary; runs of same/additive consolidate into one scene with one canonical frame
- Result: every visual change becomes a 'scene' regardless of whether the new content is informational

New behavior:
- Each candidate frame gets classified by KIND:
  - DROP: talking-head, blank/transition, title-card (chapter dividers), pull-quote-card (text duplicating transcript)
  - KEEP: diagram, map, chart, code, ui, b-roll, animated-build-step, hybrid-with-info-overlay
- pHash becomes a deduplication check (Hamming ≤ 5 between consecutive keepers → drop one) rather than a scene-boundary trigger
- Bisect the GAP between consecutive keepers when > ~15s AND the intervening transcript suggests visual content ('as you can see', 'let me show you', etc.)
- Inside an animated-build-step run: sample densely (every 2-3s) and keep ALL the steps — do not collapse to the terminal frame

Output: ordered list of (timestamp, frame_path, kind) triples for kept frames. NOT 'scenes covering the whole video' — just informational moments and where they are. Phase B (scene consolidation) is dissolved by this change.

See docs/youtube-wiki-design.md → 'The value-filter principle' for full spec.

The Phase 1 e2e validation (ih2.10) on the grain-haul test video (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MIqpvpNS5pI) surfaced four structural flaws in the synthesizer's image selection and output formatting:

- 'Auto-caption soft-correct policy' addresses the homophone-error issue

title: synthesizer: bisection + LLM judgment loop using parent agent's vision
description:
The smart core of the youtube-synthesizer skill. Replaces the relocated work from ih2.3 (vision-LLM judge) and ih2.4-original (transcript-aligned bisection pipeline).

Approach: the synthesizer is a Claude Code skill. When invoked, the parent Claude Code agent reads the SKILL.md and follows the procedure. The procedure includes opening frame images via the Read tool and reasoning over them — no separate API calls, no separate billing, just the agent's native vision capability.

Procedure:
1. Get transcript via youtube-transcript skill
2. Decide initial sample timestamps: transcript snippet boundaries + chapter starts (from yt-dlp metadata via screenshotter's --no-download metadata mode)
3. Call screenshotter extract with those timestamps; receive manifest with frames + pHashes
4. For each consecutive pair, classify by pHash band:
   - same (Hamming ≤5): drop one without further work
   - different (Hamming ≥20): keep both without further work
   - ambiguous (middle band): agent opens both image files, looks at them with the transcript span between as context, decides one of {same, different, additive}
5. For different/additive pairs where bisection might reveal more, schedule additional timestamps. Re-call screenshotter (cached frames are free to re-request)
6. Continue bisecting until terminal conditions:
   - Window <1s, OR
   - Endpoints judged 'same', OR
   - Additive build-up reaches its terminal frame (forward bisection until 'same' or 'different')
7. Emit final list of (timestamp, frame_path, transcript_span, verdict) tuples for downstream scene consolidation (ih2.6)

Bias toward false positives. Calling 'different' on actual-same wastes one frame in the wiki (cheap). Calling 'same' on actual-different loses information forever (expensive).

This issue produces:
- The SKILL.md procedure document the agent follows
- Any helper scripts the agent invokes (probably just the screenshotter extract CLI from ih2.4)

See docs/youtube-wiki-design.md (post-pivot architecture).
close_reason: Phase A of the synthesizer SKILL.md written: bisection + visual-diff judgment procedure that the parent agent follows step by step. Covers transcript+metadata fetch, initial sampling (transcript boundaries + chapters + silence anchors), pHash-based filtering with explicit thresholds and ambiguous-band visual judgment, false-positive bias guidance with transcript-context examples, bisection rules, terminal conditions, and the (timestamp, frame_path, transcript_span, verdict) tuple-list output. Phase B/C/D placeholders flagged at the bottom. Plugin scaffolding (.claude-plugin/, skills/youtube-synthesizer/) created. End-to-end validation deferred to ih2.10 — ih2.11 ships the procedure document; subsequent issues add the consolidation/extraction/writing phases on top.

title: synthesizer: output writing — folder structure, hierarchical-vs-flat, image storage
description:
Assemble all pieces and write to the target Obsidian vault.

Per-note folder (vs shared attachments/) — each entry self-contains its assets, portable.

title: screenshotter: unified extract CLI batching download + frames + pHash
description:
With the LLM judge relocated to the synthesizer (see ih2.3 close note), this issue becomes a thinner integration: expose a single 'extract' command that takes a video URL + a list of timestamps, calls the existing primitives (video.py for download/metadata, frames.py for extraction, phash.py for hashes), and emits one manifest JSON.

Manifest shape:
- video metadata block (video_id, source_url, duration, channel_id, chapter_markers, etc — once per manifest)
- entries: list of (timestamp, frame_path, phash) per requested timestamp

This is the stable interface the synthesizer's bisection loop will call — possibly multiple times during a single video processing run (initial sample + refined timestamps after judgments). The screenshotter caches frames by filename, so re-extraction at known timestamps is free.

No LLM calls in the screenshotter. No bisection logic — that lives in the synthesizer.

See docs/youtube-wiki-design.md (post-pivot architecture).

Frame cache from ih2.2 still applies, so re-requesting a known timestamp is free. Verified on grain-haul video at /tmp/yt-screenshotter-test: cached video + 2 cached frames + 1 fresh extraction completed in 2.4s. Manifest shape matches the issue spec. Sibling imports work via sys.path.insert(0, Path(__file__).parent) at the top of the script.

title: screenshotter: vision-LLM diff judge with transcript context
description:
Vision-LLM judge for ambiguous frame pairs from the pHash pre-filter. Use Claude Haiku 4.5 with two image inputs and the transcript span between the two timestamps as context.

Verdict is exactly one of:
- same: no meaningful change
- different: new content / scene change
- additive: frame B contains everything in A plus more (slide animations, code typing, equation reveals)

Bias prompt toward false positives — when uncertain, prefer different/additive. Calling 'different' on actual-same wastes one frame in the wiki (cheap). Calling 'same' on actual-different loses information forever (expensive).

Use prompt caching on the system prompt and judge schema. Per-call input is two images + a transcript snippet, so caching covers the system prompt only.

See docs/youtube-wiki-design.md for the full diff-judge design.
close_reason: Architectural pivot: vision-LLM judging moves out of the screenshotter (where it would need a separate ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and double-bill against Claude Code's existing inference) and into the synthesizer's parent-agent reasoning (which natively has vision and runs on the user's existing Claude Code session). The screenshotter becomes mechanical-only (download + frames + pHash). The bisection + judge loop becomes synthesizer orchestration logic. New issue created downstream for the relocated work. Script (judge.py) deleted from working tree before commit.

title: screenshotter: ffmpeg frame extraction at timestamps + pHash pre-filter
description:
Given a downloaded video and a list of timestamps, extract frames as PNGs via ffmpeg. Implement perceptual-hash diffing (pHash via imagehash or equivalent) for cheap pre-filtering.

Define three bands via Hamming distance thresholds: very-similar → declare 'same' without LLM call; very-different → declare 'different' without LLM call; ambiguous middle band → defer to vision LLM judge (separate issue). Thresholds tunable via constants. Expect 60-80% of frame pairs decided by pHash alone in practice.

See docs/youtube-wiki-design.md for sampling design.
close_reason: Two scripts: frames.py extracts video frames at given timestamps via ffmpeg (filename-based cache so re-extraction is ~free); phash.py provides three-band classifier (same/different/ambiguous) on 64-bit pHash Hamming distance with default thresholds SAME_MAX=5, DIFFERENT_MIN=20 (tunable kwargs). Verified on grain-haul video: 7 frames extracted, 7 pair comparisons exhibit the full verdict spectrum, cached re-extraction is ~100ms for 3 frames. Imports: 'from frames import extract_frames'; 'from phash import compute, classify_pair'.

title: screenshotter: yt-dlp video download + metadata extraction
description:
Foundation layer for youtube-screenshotter skill. Download video to a working directory and capture all metadata fields needed for downstream synthesis: channel name, channel_id (stable across renames), title, source_published_date, duration_seconds, chapter_markers, playlist_id, playlist_title.

Use yt-dlp's --dump-json to extract metadata in one pass. Target 720p resolution to balance quality vs. download time and disk usage. Cleanup behavior: keep video file for the duration of one synthesizer run (frames are extracted from it), delete after.

See docs/youtube-wiki-design.md for context.
close_reason: Foundation script video.py downloads at 720p and emits all required metadata (channel, channel_id, title, source_published_date, duration_seconds, chapter_markers, playlist_id, playlist_title). Verified end-to-end against MIqpvpNS5pI: 56MB MP4 at 1280x720, all 8 metadata fields populated, 4 chapter markers detected, 5s wall time. --no-download flag also added for cheap metadata-only iteration. Importable from siblings as 'from video import download'.

title: youtube-wiki: extract searchable knowledge from YouTube videos into Obsidian vaults
description:
Three-skill pipeline turning YouTube videos (explainers, howtos, lectures) into Obsidian literature notes for a personal searchable corpus. Produces faithful capture (transcript + canonical visual scenes + structured extraction), leaving the user's synthesis layer free to weave content downstream.

See docs/youtube-wiki-design.md for the full architectural design, frontmatter schema, sampling strategy, and phasing.

Phase 1 (this epic): transcript + new screenshotter + new synthesizer producing in-place wiki entries with embedded video frames. Test target: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MIqpvpNS5pI (grain-haul economics, visuals load-bearing).

Future phases captured in design doc:
- Phase 2: nano-banana imagery regen for publishable / copyright-safe output
- Phase 3: youtube-comments skill + integration
- Phase 4+: sqlite3 corpus indexer, cross-source linker, parallel source-skills (articles, pdfs, substack)
close_reason: All 11 child issues closed. Phase 1 of the youtube-wiki epic is complete: youtube-transcript (already shipped), youtube-screenshotter (mechanical primitives + extract CLI + plugin scaffold + marketplace registration), and youtube-synthesizer (4-phase SKILL.md procedure: bisection+visual-diff judgment, scene consolidation+frontmatter, structured extraction, output writing) all live on main. End-to-end validation against the grain-haul test video produced a clean hierarchical Obsidian entry (see ih2.10 close note). Architectural pivot during build: vision-LLM judging moved from a Python subprocess (which would need a separate ANTHROPIC_API_KEY) to the synthesizer's parent-agent reasoning (which uses the user's existing Claude Code session — no double-billing). Phase 2 (nano-banana imagery regen for publishable output), Phase 3 (youtube-comments), and Phase 4+ (sqlite3 corpus indexer + cross-source linker + parallel source-skills) remain in docs/youtube-wiki-design.md as future work.

title: pr-review-loop: defensive handling for unclosed code fence in AGENT-REVIEWERS.md awk parsers
description:
The fence-aware awk programs in pr-review-loop close one specific bug class (H1/H2 inside closed fences silently dropping agents / config). They do NOT handle a related class: malformed input with an UNCLOSED fence.

## Behavior gap

In both _parse_configuration.sh and discover-agents.sh parse_agent_file, `in_fence` toggles on every `^\`\`\`` line but never resets on H1 boundaries or EOF. If a user's AGENT-REVIEWERS.md has an unclosed code fence in one section, every subsequent H1/H2 falls into the catch-all content branch and gets silently swallowed into the prior section.

Same silent-data-loss symptom as the fence-unaware bug class — just triggered by malformed input rather than fenced headings.

## Proposed fix

Options (pick one — design discussion):

1. **Auto-close at EOF with stderr warning** — in the awk `END` block, if `in_fence` is still 1, print a warning to stderr (`Warning: unclosed code fence in <file>`). Easy. Doesn't recover the dropped content.

2. **Auto-close at H1 with stderr warning** — if an `^# ` line appears inside `in_fence`, AND `in_fence` has been open for >N lines, force-close it and warn. Heuristic, fragile.

3. **Fail the parse** — exit non-zero with a clear "unclosed fence" diagnostic. Cleanest. But blocks the loop on a typo.

Recommendation: option 1 (warn at EOF) — preserves loop progress, surfaces the issue clearly.

## Surfaced by

pr-test-analyzer agent finding in the downstream review loop on PR #31 (fence-unaware awk bug class sweep). The reviewer flagged this as criticality 7 but the scope is a different bug class than #31 covers, so triaged out-of-scope and tracked here.

## Acceptance

- _parse_configuration.sh and discover-agents.sh parse_agent_file both emit a stderr warning when EOF is reached with `in_fence != 0`.
- Tests added covering the warning path (no behavior beyond stderr output).

title: pr-review-loop: per-agent validator_model override in AGENT-REVIEWERS.md
description:
## Why

Once the validator-independence fix lands (drop implicit model-mirroring; default all validators to sonnet for clean per-flagger acceptance-rate telemetry), some users will want to opt specific agents into a stronger validator (opus for nuanced bug-class findings) or a cheaper one (haiku for high-volume style nitpick agents).

The pre-fix model-mirror behavior was implicit and category-derived — surfaced as a contamination of per-flagger telemetry during external review. This ticket replaces the implicit behavior with an explicit opt-in: users add 'validator_model: opus' (or sonnet/haiku) to a specific agent's AGENT-REVIEWERS.md entry, accepting the telemetry trade-off for THAT agent only.

## Design

### Config shape

Per-agent in 'AGENT-REVIEWERS.md':

```markdown
## security-reviewer

validator_model: opus

[reviewer body...]
```

Or via frontmatter / YAML block — to be decided during implementation. The discover-agents.sh output schema gains a 'validator_model' field on each agent (null when unset).

### Default

When 'validator_model' is unset on an agent: use the global default ('sonnet'). When set: use that model.

### Validation

Accepted values: 'sonnet', 'opus', 'haiku'. Reject other values with a clear error at discover-agents.sh parse time.

### Telemetry documentation

In SKILL.md '#### Telemetry' section, add a one-paragraph caveat:

> Per-flagger acceptance rate is comparable across agents that share the same validator model. If you override validator_model on an agent, its acceptance rate is no longer directly comparable to other agents' — the rate reflects both flagger quality and the chosen validator's strictness. This is by design: you opted into the trade-off for this agent.

### Spawning summary

The pre-round summary (currently 'Validation: enabled') should mention overrides:

> Validation: enabled. Overrides: security-reviewer→opus, comment-analyzer→haiku.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Per-agent 'validator_model' field parseable from AGENT-REVIEWERS.md
- [ ] discover-agents.sh output includes 'validator_model' per agent (null when unset)
- [ ] Validator Task spawn uses the agent's overridden model when set, sonnet otherwise
- [ ] Parser rejects invalid model names
- [ ] SKILL.md telemetry section documents the caveat
- [ ] Spawning summary mentions overrides

## Dependencies

Blocked on the upstream validator-independence fix that establishes sonnet as the global default (drops implicit category-based mirroring).

title: youtube-synthesizer: add test harness for sync_to_dropbox.sh path-shape validation
description:
PR #28 review (round 1) surfaced from pr-test-analyzer: sync_to_dropbox.sh has no test framework, yet wraps a destructive rclone operation. Path-shape validation logic now branches on SOURCE_TYPE (video vs book), but a silent regression that miscomputes TARGET, PARENT_REMOTE, or accidentally widens the case statement could cause data loss in Dropbox.

## Suggested test cases

### Happy paths (must succeed-and-call-rclone-with-correct-target)
- Video entry: `~/ObsidianVaults/V/sources/videos/C/E/` → TARGET=`dropbox:ObsidianVaults/V/sources/videos/C/E/`
- Book leaf: `~/ObsidianVaults/V/sources/books/B/` → TARGET=`dropbox:ObsidianVaults/V/sources/books/B/`

### Negative paths (must fail-closed BEFORE invoking rclone)
- Wrong vault root: `/tmp/foo/sources/videos/C/E/` → exit 1 with prefix-mismatch error
- Too few components (videos shape): `~/ObsidianVaults/V/sources/videos/` → exit 1
- Too few components (books shape): `~/ObsidianVaults/V/sources/` → exit 1
- Wrong middle component: `~/ObsidianVaults/V/sources/podcasts/X/` → exit 1 (unrecognized shape)
- Empty inner components: `~/ObsidianVaults//sources/videos/C/E/` → exit 1 (vault name empty)

### Defense-in-depth (must catch too-broad targets)
- Force TARGET = `dropbox:` somehow (mock-friendly entry path) → case statement should reject
- Force TARGET = `dropbox:ObsidianVaults/V/sources/videos/` → case statement should reject
- Force TARGET = `dropbox:ObsidianVaults/V/sources/books/` → $PARENT_REMOTE-arm of case should reject

### Mocking rclone
The tests will need a fake `rclone` on PATH (or PATH override) that just records what args it was called with, so we can assert (a) it was called with the expected target and (b) it was NOT called when the script should have refused.

## Refs
- PR #28: https://github.com/devonjones/devon-claude-skills/pull/28
- pr-test-analyzer round-1 comment: https://github.com/devonjones/devon-claude-skills/pull/28#discussion_r3282956382
- Tracker ref for parent work: this is a follow-up that supports beads tickets devon-claude-skills-44z + -7h0 (youtube-synthesizer book-leaf rollout)

title: fv0: add 3 deferred test cases (TF tiebreaker, fence content roundtrip, Gemfile in pruned dir)
description:
Three test coverage gaps surfaced by pr-test-analyzer during PR #27's review loop. All criticality 4-6 (should-test, not blocking-gap). Deferred from PR #27 to keep that PR focused on the templates + fence-aware fix.

## Tests to add (in plugins/pr-review-loop/skills/pr-review-loop/tests/test-language-templates.sh)

### T1 (criticality 6) — Terraform priority tiebreaker
detect-language.sh:71-75 encodes priorities 0-4 across the 5 Terraform manifests so unique_by([.language, .subtree]) picks main.tf as canonical. No existing test creates main.tf + versions.tf + providers.tf in the same dir to verify the priority sort works. A regression that swapped the priority numbers would silently change which manifest is reported.

Suggested test 'detect_terraform_picks_main_tf_when_multiple_tf_files_coexist': create all 3 files in same dir; assert exactly one entry and .[0].manifest == 'main.tf'.

Source comment: https://github.com/devonjones/devon-claude-skills/pull/27#discussion_r2208860

### T2 (criticality 5) — Fence content roundtrip
install-template.sh:196-200 fence-aware extract_section does `if (in_section) print` on the fence line so code blocks are kept in the section body. I_FA1-I_FA5 verify the COUNT of '## ' agent headings; they do not assert that the BODY of code blocks round-trips into the installed file. If someone refactored to `next` without `print`, agent count would still pass but every code example would silently vanish.

Suggested test 'install_preserves_code_block_content_inside_extracted_section': install python:., grep installed AGENT-REVIEWERS.md for a distinctive substring from a code example inside the # Agents section (e.g. a Python docstring quoted in one of the GOOD/BAD examples). Assert match present.

### T3 (criticality 4) — Gemfile inside pruned dir
detect-language.sh:82 (Gemfile entry) + skip_dirs list includes 'vendor'. D8 verifies node_modules pruning for go.mod, but no equivalent test ensures a Gemfile inside vendor/ (or .git/) is pruned. A subtle change to the prune expression would falsely flag every consumer of a rails gem as a rails repo themselves.

Suggested test 'detect_skips_gemfile_in_vendor_dir': create vendor/some-gem/Gemfile with `gem "rails"`; assert detect-language.sh output is [].

## Refs

- PR #27: https://github.com/devonjones/devon-claude-skills/pull/27
- pr-test-analyzer comment T1: https://github.com/devonjones/devon-claude-skills/pull/27#discussion_r3282272976
- pr-test-analyzer comment T2: https://github.com/devonjones/devon-claude-skills/pull/27#discussion_r3282274289
- pr-test-analyzer comment T3: https://github.com/devonjones/devon-claude-skills/pull/27#discussion_r3282274816
notes:
Round-2 addition: new test T4 needed for the widened Gemfile regex.

### T4 (criticality 5) — Widened Gemfile regex syntax variants

PR #27 commit 804beb6 widened the Gemfile regex from `gem[[:space:]]+['"]rails['"]` to `gem[[:space:]]*\(?[[:space:]]*['"]rails['"]` specifically to accept both `gem("rails")` (paren syntax) and `gem 'rails'` (single-quoted) in addition to the existing `gem "rails"`. D11 only exercises the double-quoted no-paren form.

A regression dropping the `\(?` or narrowing the quote class would silently fail to detect Rails repos using either non-default style — exactly the false-negative the fix was meant to prevent.

Suggested test 'detect_rails_via_gemfile_with_paren_and_single_quote_syntax': two assertions in one test:
(a) Gemfile containing `gem('rails', '~> 7.1')` → detects rails
(b) Gemfile containing `gem 'rails'` (single-quoted, no parens) → detects rails

### Source
- pr-test-analyzer round-2 comment: https://github.com/devonjones/devon-claude-skills/pull/27#discussion_r3282407289

title: pr-review-loop: switch discover-agents.sh from --argjson to --slurpfile to avoid MAX_ARG_STRLEN
description:
Surfaced by Gemini Code Assist on PR #18 (medium severity). discover-agents.sh constructs its jq input via  which embeds the entire JSON in the command line. On Linux MAX_ARG_STRLEN is typically 128KB. Current usage is ~45KB (6 defaults + 4 user agents at typical prompt sizes), but a project with many long-prompt agents could exceed the limit and get an unhelpful 'Argument list too long' error.

## Fix

Switch the JQ_INPUT construction to use temp files + jq --slurpfile (or use stdin redirection + read variables in the jq script). Specifically:

1. Write DEFAULTS_JSON, CONFIGURATION_JSON, sections, files to separate temp files via mktemp
2. Use 
3. Add temp files to a trap-based cleanup at script start
4. Update the merge jq pipeline to use the slurp'd arrays

## Reference

PR #18 line comment from gemini-code-assist on discover-agents.sh:261:
'Passing potentially large JSON strings via --argjson can hit the system\'s maximum argument length limit (MAX_ARG_STRLEN). If the combined size of the agent sections or defaults exceeds this limit (typically 128KB on Linux), the jq command will fail to execute.'

## Why P3, not higher

- Current usage is well under 128KB
- No user has hit this in practice yet
- Failure mode is loud (Argument list too long) when it does hit
- Defensive refactor; small but not blocking the bfd ship

Filed as follow-up so this PR can land while the refactor is done deliberately.

title: pr-review-loop: set up Gemini Code Assist on devon-claude-skills (for self-dogfooding)
description:
When dogfooding pr-review-loop on PR #18 (the bfd default-specialists work), Gemini Code Assist did not auto-review on PR creation because the repo has no .gemini/config.yaml. The 'all bots in each round' loop structure designed in SKILL.md was effectively reduced to 'agent reviewers only' for that PR.

For our own dogfooding to exercise the full multi-bot path, this repo needs:

1. .gemini/config.yaml with code_review: true (per the existing trigger-review.sh detection)
2. Gemini Code Assist GitHub App installed on the repo (or org-level access)
3. Verify the auto-review trigger fires on PR creation (no manual /gemini review needed for first review)

## Why P3, not higher

The agent reviewers caught 19 substantive findings in round 1 of the bfd dogfood. The skill works fine without Gemini for this repo. Gemini adds a different perspective (its model differs from Claude); worth adding but not blocking.

## Acceptance

- .gemini/config.yaml committed at repo root with code_review: true
- A test PR's creation triggers a Gemini auto-review (verify by opening any small follow-up PR)
- trigger-review.sh on this repo detects the config and waits for auto-review instead of posting /gemini review (per the existing two-check logic in the script)

## Related

- The Cowork-restricted-VM-via-MCP work in devon-claude-skills-lyy is orthogonal — that's about transport when gh is blocked. Gemini auto-review is GitHub-side and works regardless of how the loop talks to GitHub.
- The polish-and-upstream sync to valid-Eval may already have a Gemini config there; check before duplicating effort.

Surfaced by the bfd dogfood loop on PR #18 (round 1 noted Gemini did not fire).

title: pr-review-loop: add test for hierarchical subdir AGENT-REVIEWERS.md interaction with default merge
description:
Surfaced by pr-test-analyzer reviewer on PR #18 (bfd dogfood). The existing hierarchical-scope mechanism in discover-agents.sh handles AGENT-REVIEWERS.md files at multiple directory depths. bfd's C+E merge layer adds defaults on top. The interaction between the two is non-trivial and untested:

- Subdir AGENT-REVIEWERS.md with custom agent — does it spawn correctly with scope set to the subdir?
- Subdir AGENT-REVIEWERS.md overriding a default by name — does the override apply only within the subdir, or repo-wide?
- Subdir AGENT-REVIEWERS.md with # Configuration — already covered (warning + ignored), but verify behavior persists with the merge layer

## What to build

Add 2-3 test scenarios to tests/test-discover-agents.sh:

1. Subdir AGENT-REVIEWERS.md with new custom agent → spawns with scope set to /subdir/; defaults still spawn at scope /
2. Subdir AGENT-REVIEWERS.md with same-name as default → currently the merge runs once over all user_agents_raw, which means a subdir override of a default applies REPO-WIDE (replaces the root default everywhere). Verify this is the intended behavior, OR fix to per-scope override (open design question)
3. Default-agent scope is currently always /. When a subdir override exists, should the default's scope narrow? Probably NOT — defaults stay at /, subdir override takes its declared scope. Lock this in with a test.

## Out of scope (this ticket)

- Reworking the merge to support per-scope default overrides — that's a separate design question
- Anything outside tests/test-discover-agents.sh

## Reference

PR #18 line comment: https://github.com/devonjones/devon-claude-skills/pull/18#discussion_r3244847748 (pr-test-analyzer criticality 6)

title: Backport: get-pr-comments.sh script extraction
description:
Backport extraction of `get-pr-comments.sh` script — replaces inline `gh pr view --json comments | jq` invocations.

## What to lift

The `scripts/get-pr-comments.sh` script from downstream (full source — fetch from valid-Eval/valid-eval-skills/skills/pr-review-loop/scripts/). Should support:

- `<PR>` positional arg
- `--with-ids` flag to include comment IDs
- `--wait` flag for polling (up to 20 minutes per ticket 8)
- `--author <login>` filter (referenced in their SKILL.md line 100: `scripts/get-pr-comments.sh <PR> --with-ids --author claude`)

## Why

Inline `gh pr view --json comments --jq '...'` invocations are brittle, hard to read, and inconsistent across rounds. Extracting to a script gives a single canonical path for fetching PR-level comments, parallel to our existing `get-review-comments.sh` for line comments.

## Acceptance

- Script copied (with scrubbing per `downstream-bidirectional-sync-and-scrub-rule` — likely no scrub needed for a bash/jq utility)
- Permission-script behavior consistent with our other scripts (no `eval`, no compound commands, full-path-compatible)
- SKILL.md updated to reference `scripts/get-pr-comments.sh` everywhere we currently inline `gh pr view --json comments`
- Permission setup section updated to include the new script

## Note

Check if we already have a similar script — `get-pr-comments.sh` was filed in the past but I'd like to confirm before duplicating.

title: Backport: 20-minute --wait polling (was 5 minutes)
description:
Backport the polling timeout change: 5 minutes → 20 minutes for `--wait` flags.

## What to lift

Update all instances of "polls every 30s for up to 5 minutes" to "polls every 30s for up to 20 minutes" (or equivalent). Affects:

- `--wait` flag documentation in scripts
- "Step 1" / "C1" Gemini-comment-polling instructions
- Any other reference to wait timeout

## Why

Real-world Gemini response time often exceeds 5 minutes for non-trivial PRs. The 5-minute cap caused premature "no comments yet" exits requiring manual retry. Downstream production usage settled on 20 minutes.

## Acceptance

- All "5 minute" / "5 minutes" / "5min" references in --wait context updated to 20
- Confirm the actual scripts (e.g., `scripts/get-review-comments.sh`) implement 20-minute polling — if hardcoded shorter, file follow-up to align them
- No business-specific language to scrub here

## Note

Tiny ticket but real lift — this single number causes flaky behavior in our current version.

title: Backport: Evaluating Suggestions on Instruction Documents (SKILL.md self-modification rules)
description:
Backport "Evaluating Suggestions on Instruction Documents" subsection from downstream SKILL.md.

## What to lift

Special evaluation rules for PRs that modify SKILL.md, agent playbooks, reference guides, or runbooks. Central test:

> **Ask: does removing this content change how an agent or human will behave when following these instructions?**

Supporting rules:

- **Reinforcement earns its length.** Repetition that drives agents toward the correct path is worth keeping even when it "duplicates" something said elsewhere
- **Behavioral signal > character count.** Word choice matters — "Recommended as the default operational posture" ≠ "the default"
- **Expected-output anchors are instruction, not changelog.** "Expected output: 0 findings for X, multiple for Y" is instruction; remove date/repo attribution, keep expected result
- **Transition sentences that restate headings add noise.** Test: does any agent behavior change if this sentence is absent? If not, cut it
- **When in doubt, preserve the content and note as "Won't fix" with behavioral reasoning explanation**

## Why

PR-review-loop reviews ITS OWN SKILL.md. Agents applying "remove verbose duplication" rules naively will gut behavioral reinforcement that earns its length. This section catches the self-referential failure mode.

## Acceptance

- Subsection added under our "Be Skeptical of Reviews" area
- Scrubbed per bd memory `downstream-bidirectional-sync-and-scrub-rule`
- Test phrasing ("does removing this content change behavior?") kept verbatim — it's the forcing function
- Cross-reference: link from our clarity-reviewer agent guidance, since clarity-reviewer is the most likely agent to flag verbose-but-load-bearing instruction text

title: Backport: Evidence Gates for High-Risk Changes (6-category table)
description:
Backport "Evidence Gates for High-Risk Changes" table from downstream SKILL.md.

## What to lift

The full table with 6 change categories + required evidence per category:

| Change Category | Evidence Required |
|----------------|-------------------|
| Changing test factories (e.g., `create` → `build_stubbed`) | Verify the test doesn't depend on persistence (database queries, associations, callbacks) |
| Replacing API calls with "simpler" alternatives | Verify identical semantics via library documentation — not assumptions |
| Removing code that appears unused (placeholders, explicit branches) | Check if it serves a structural purpose (namespacing, extension points, readability) |
| Changing eager evaluation to short-circuit | Confirm no side effects depend on evaluating all branches |
| Modifying database migrations | Verify against the library's official migration templates |
| Changing log levels | Confirm the original level isn't intentional for production observability |

Plus the surrounding paragraph: "If evidence is ambiguous, preserve the original implementation and flag it as a question."

## Why

Production-derived list of categories where bots have been observed to break working code by applying changes without checking the structural reasoning. Strong "show your work" forcing function.

## Acceptance

- Table lifted into our "Be Skeptical of Reviews" area, near the "Respect Human Design Decisions" subsection (ticket 5)
- `create → build_stubbed` is factory_bot/Rails-flavored — keep but neutralize wording so it reads as language-agnostic example ("e.g., factory `create` → `build_stubbed` in Ruby on Rails, or analogous patterns in your stack")
- Scrubbed per bd memory `downstream-bidirectional-sync-and-scrub-rule`
- Cross-reference from agent-spawn prompt template so agents know to invoke evidence gates

title: Backport: Respect Human Design Decisions (concrete operating steps)
description:
Backport "Respect Human Design Decisions" subsection from downstream SKILL.md, extending our "Critical: Be Skeptical of Reviews" section.

## What to lift

Four numbered operating steps for when bot suggestions conflict with human-approved patterns:

1. **Read PR comments and documentation** — human reviewers often explain reasoning; check before applying contradicting bot suggestions
2. **Verify against library documentation** — bots sometimes hallucinate APIs or misunderstand library behavior
3. **Weigh the magnitude** — only override human-approved patterns for concrete, significant issues (correctness, security, measurable performance), not stylistic preferences
4. **Ask rather than assert** — when uncertain whether a pattern is intentional, flag as a question rather than silently changing

## Why

Our current "Critical: Be Skeptical of Reviews" section is one paragraph + bullet list. Downstream extended this into concrete operating steps after observing the failure mode "bot says do X, agent does X, breaks human-approved design."

## Acceptance

- Subsection added under our "Critical: Be Skeptical of Reviews" section
- Cross-reference to the broader skeptical-of-reviews framing preserved
- Scrubbed per bd memory `downstream-bidirectional-sync-and-scrub-rule`
- Connects with "Respect existing patterns" if we have any other such section

title: pr-review-loop: documented structured-prompt template for AGENT-REVIEWERS.md (focus/check/flag/skip/suggest)
description:
Document a structured prompt template for AGENT-REVIEWERS.md entries (and apply it to the 6 native specialists shipped via bfd + 9ao). Currently the AGENT-REVIEWERS.md system accepts free-form prose under each `# Agents` H2 heading; this works but yields inconsistent agent quality.

**Treat as a spike per bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`.** Configurable in the sense that user-authored agents that DON'T follow the template still work — we recommend, don't enforce.

## The template

From valid-Eval/valid-eval-skills/AGENT-REVIEWERS.md (downstream's pattern, which they evolved independently and is materially better than free-form prose):

```markdown
## <agent-name>

You are a reviewer ensuring <one-line purpose>.

**Your focus:** <one paragraph scoping the agent's domain>

**What to check:**
1. <numbered specific checks>
2. ...

**Flag issues if:**
- <bullet of specific flag conditions>
- ...

**Do NOT flag:**
- <bullet of explicit non-issues — covers nitpicks, pre-existing-but-not-touched, stylistic preferences, etc.>

**When flagging, suggest:**
- <what the fix should look like / where to put it>
```

This structure forces the author to articulate (a) the agent's scope, (b) positive criteria, (c) negative criteria, (d) actionability — the four things weak agent definitions universally miss.

## What to do

1. **Document the template** in SKILL.md (or a references file once we do the references-pattern refactor) — under the AGENT-REVIEWERS.md section. Recommend without enforcing; show before/after examples of converting a free-form agent to the structured form.
2. **Apply the template** to the 6 (then 8 with 9ao) native specialists shipped via bfd. Each gets:
   - Your focus
   - What to check
   - Flag issues if
   - Do NOT flag (with touch-it-you-own-it scope rules from rb7 baked in)
   - When flagging, suggest
3. **Update q2h** (the "audit AGENT-REVIEWERS.md against defaults" ticket) to also evaluate user agents against the template — if a user's agent is free-form prose, suggest the structured rewrite.

## Why this is small but high-leverage

- Forces every agent to declare what it WON'T flag, not just what it will. Solves the "agents over-flag noise" problem at the prompt level, before our filter stack (rb7/ydy/0nl) even sees the output.
- Aligns user-authored agents with our touched-vs-untouched scope rules (rb7) by default — the "Do NOT flag" section is the natural home for "issues in files outside changed_files scope" etc.
- Makes agent definitions easier to read, diff, and review in PRs.

## Interaction with other tickets

- **bfd** (6 defaults) — author all 6 in this template format
- **9ao** (security + performance) — author both in this template format
- **q2h** (audit existing AGENT-REVIEWERS.md against defaults) — extend audit to recommend structural rewrites
- **rb7** (touch-it-you-own-it) — the template's "Do NOT flag" section is the natural place to encode scope rules per agent

## Reference

valid-Eval/valid-eval-skills/AGENT-REVIEWERS.md — particularly `marketplace-reviewer`, `dependency-reviewer`, `shell-script-reviewer`. The shell-script-reviewer entry is the strongest example because it explicitly handles the touched-vs-untouched distinction:

> A **modified existing** script is missing `set -euo pipefail` — flag as a pre-existing gap to address in a follow-up PR, not a blocker for this PR

This is touch-it-you-own-it in agent-prompt form. We can codify this pattern across all our defaults.

## Acceptance

- Template documented in our SKILL.md (or references/) with example before/after
- All 6 (or 8) native specialists rewritten in template form
- q2h ticket extended to recommend structural rewrites for free-form user agents
- Spike marker: this is a recommendation, not enforcement; free-form agents still work
close_reason: Invalid — the 'discovered' structured prompt template (focus/check/flag/skip/suggest) was already our own pattern in AGENT-REVIEWERS.md, just reflected in the downstream fork. No real new content here. Filed in error after misreading downstream as upstream of the pattern.

title: Epic: pr-review-loop ← wshobson/agents code-reviewer learnings
description:
Harvest the one new idea from wshobson/agents code-reviewer (https://github.com/wshobson/agents — note original `development/code-reviewer.md` URL is stale; canonical copies now live at `plugins/*/agents/code-reviewer.md`).

## Read of the upstream

Eighth peer skill audited. Two variants:

- **Canonical** (171 lines, 6 byte-identical copies under different plugins): mostly aspirational capability-listing — declarative "I can use SonarQube/CodeQL/Semgrep/Snyk/Bandit/OWASP/etc." with no scripts, no `allowed-tools`, no orchestration. The agent doesn't actually invoke any of those tools. Persona-bias prompt, not a functional skill.
- **Incident-response variant** (32 lines): terse, focused, more useful. Contains the one new idea.

## What we are taking

- **devon-claude-skills-msb** (P3, spike): codebase-wide pattern detection for similar bug instances. When an agent finds a bug, grep the codebase for the same pattern; touched-file occurrences flagged normally, untouched-file occurrences captured as follow-up beads tickets (reconciles with touch-it-you-own-it).

## Refinements to existing tickets

- **9ao (security-reviewer)** — could borrow the "production reliability" framing tone (emphasize incidents/downtime/breaches as motivating context rather than abstract correctness). Minor enhancement, not worth a separate ticket update.

## Skip — not useful

- Capability-listing format itself — verbose, aspirational, not functional. Our constrained specialists are more effective.
- External-AI-tool name-drops (Trag/Bito/Codiga/Copilot) — performative; doesn't change behavior in a Claude prompt.
- "Configuration & infrastructure review" as a distinct specialist — overlaps with 9ao + vsf, not worth a 9th agent.
- Mentioning SonarQube/CodeQL/Semgrep — the idea of running them is already captured by dl6 (deterministic pre-checks).
- Slack/Teams integration, onboarding/training — out of scope.

## Diminishing returns observation

This audit yields exactly 1 borderline ticket. Combined with the prior three audits (presmihaylov, udecode, MiniMax-AI — 1 ticket each), the trend is clear: **the audit set has saturated**. After this epic, future peer-skill audits should be opportunistic (when a skill is materially different architecturally), not exhaustive.

## Spike framing

Per bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`, msb is configurable, default-OFF, telemetry-emitting via gx4. We don't know yet whether the extra grep work produces real follow-up tickets or wasteful noise. Real-world data will tell.

title: pr-review-loop: codebase-wide pattern detection for similar bug instances (beads tickets for untouched-file occurrences)
description:
When an agent reviewer finds a bug or vulnerability, it should additionally grep the codebase for the same pattern at other call sites and check whether they have the same issue. Touched-file occurrences flagged normally; untouched-file occurrences captured as follow-up beads tickets.

**Treat as a spike per bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`.** Configurable per agent; default OFF until we know whether the extra search is worth the tokens it costs.

## Why

Bugs rarely exist alone. A SQL-injection-via-string-concat in one query usually means there are similar patterns elsewhere. A missing null check on `.toString()` is often replicated across dozens of call sites. Currently our agents are scoped to `changed_files` and don't search beyond — so when a real pattern bug surfaces, we fix the one occurrence and miss the rest.

## Reference

wshobson/agents incident-response variant of code-reviewer:

> Pattern detection: find similar vulnerabilities across the codebase

The full wshobson skill is mostly aspirational persona text, but the pattern-detection idea is real and missing from our current setup.

## What to build

### Per-finding pattern search

When an agent reviewer emits a finding, optionally extend the search:

1. **Extract pattern signature** from the finding — e.g. the regex/AST snippet that characterizes the bug. Agent does this itself in its reasoning.
2. **Grep the codebase** for the same signature (using existing Grep tool, scoped to the agent's normal language/file filters).
3. **Filter results** to UNTOUCHED files (i.e., not in PR's `changed_files`).
4. **Classify each hit** as: same-bug-applies / similar-but-different / false-positive (agent judgment, possibly via brief Read of the surrounding context).

### Reconciliation with touch-it-you-own-it (rb7 + bd memory `code-review-philosophy-when-you-touch-code-you`)

- **Touched-file occurrences** → flag normally via the agent's standard pipeline (rb7 + ydy + 0nl, batched via 77j)
- **Untouched-file occurrences that the agent classifies as same-bug** → file a beads ticket via the existing out-of-scope flow:
  - Title: "<bug type> pattern: <N> additional occurrences outside this PR's scope"
  - Description: list each occurrence with file:line, the matching code snippet, why it's same-bug, plus a link back to the originating PR finding
  - This gives us the signal without violating the touched-vs-untouched scope rule
- **Untouched-file occurrences classified as different / false-positive** → drop silently; not worth even a beads ticket

### Cost guards

- **Budget per agent per round**: max N pattern searches (default 3 — high-severity findings only). Don't let an agent grep 50 patterns and balloon the round time.
- **Severity gate**: only do pattern search for ⚠️ Major or 🚨 Critical findings (per 5zk's severity dimension). 📝 Minor and 💡 Suggestions don't earn the extra work.
- **Telemetry** (gx4): emit a record per pattern search showing `pattern, files_grepped, hits_found, same_bug_count, beads_tickets_filed`. Lets us evaluate whether the extra work is producing real follow-up tickets vs. noise.

## Interaction with other tickets

- **rb7** (do-not-flag list) — already says "issues in files outside the PR's changed_files scope" should not be flagged. This ticket extends that: untouched-file occurrences of the SAME bug get a beads ticket instead, not silent drop.
- **9ao** (security-reviewer), **0nl** (brainstorm-then-narrow), **ydy** (validator) — orthogonal; this is an OUTPUT augmentation, not a different filter.
- **5zk** (severity prefix) — used to gate which findings earn pattern search.
- **gx4** (telemetry) — new field group for pattern-search records.

## Acceptance

- Agents can optionally invoke pattern search post-finding (configurable per agent in their subagent frontmatter or in AGENT-REVIEWERS.md)
- Touched-file occurrences flagged normally
- Untouched-file same-bug occurrences captured as beads tickets via existing out-of-scope flow
- Budget cap enforced (default 3 pattern searches per agent per round)
- Telemetry records pattern-search outcomes
- Default OFF — opt-in per agent until we have data on whether it earns its cost (per bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`)

title: Epic: pr-review-loop ← presmihaylov github-code-reviewer skill learnings
description:
Harvest patterns from presmihaylov/claude github-code-reviewer skill (https://github.com/presmihaylov/claude/tree/master/.claude/skills/github-code-reviewer) into pr-review-loop.

## Read of the upstream

The simplest of the five peer skills reviewed. Single-shot review (no loop), three Python scripts (get_pr_info, get_pr_diff, submit_review), one example JSON, SKILL.md and README. Stance: very aggressive filter — "high signal, low noise; skip everything else."

Architecturally minimal. The interesting parts are the conventions (comment format) and the reference implementation (Python pending-review scripts).

## What we are taking

- **devon-claude-skills-5zk** (P3, spike): adopt the prescriptive `category: issue` 1-2 line comment format. Per-agent default category. Configurable on/off, telemetry-emitting per gx4.
- **Refinement to devon-claude-skills-77j**: make the review-event policy configurable (named presets: strict_comment_only / severity_based / always_approve_when_clean) rather than hardcoded. Also references presmihaylov's `submit_review.py` as the reference implementation pattern for the pending-review batched-review script.

## Philosophy conflict — flagged, NOT taken

presmihaylov skips test coverage gaps unless they're on "critical paths." This conflicts with our touch-it-you-own-it stance (rb7 + bd memory `code-review-philosophy-when-you-touch-code-you`): test coverage gaps in touched files are in scope under our policy. We do NOT adopt their stance.

## Other things they do that we already do (or do better)

- Pending-review API for batched comments — already planned (77j)
- Don't post when no findings — already our default behavior
- `gh` over MCP — already our chosen path (ypj closed, bd memory)
- Inline comments on specific lines — already our model (post-line-comment.sh)

## What we skip

- "Submit as COMMENT only — never APPROVE or REQUEST_CHANGES." Now configurable in 77j; their stance is one of the named presets.
- Aggressive style/refactoring skip — refactoring is handled by code-simplifier (gzk), not by primary review agents.

## Spike framing

Per bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`, treat 5zk and the 77j event-policy refinement as experiments. The category format may help skim-ability or may distort findings; we measure via gx4 telemetry rather than committing.

## Children

- devon-claude-skills-5zk (P3): category-prefix comment format

(plus a refinement appended to devon-claude-skills-77j in the aidankinzett epic — that one stays parented to 37s, the refinement is cross-epic.)

title: pr-review-loop: prescriptive 'category: issue' (1-2 line) comment format with per-agent category
description:
Adopt presmihaylov's prescriptive `category: issue` comment format across all agent reviewer findings, so bot output has a consistent skimmable shape.

**Treat as a spike per bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`.** Configurable on/off, telemetry-emitting (per gx4) so we can measure whether the consistent format actually improves review velocity or just adds friction.

## The format

1-2 lines max. Category prefix maps to specialist agent or finding type:

```
bug: Null pointer exception if response.data is undefined
security: SQL injection - use parameterized queries instead of string concatenation
performance: N+1 query - fetch all items with single query instead of loop
breaking: API response structure changed - will break existing clients
arch: Service layer calling external API directly - should go through repository
type: Returns `any` for value that's known to be User | null
test: New code path in handlePayment() has no test coverage (touched file)
docs: Public API method has no docstring
```

Categories proposed (one per specialist + a generic):

| Category | Default agent |
|----------|---------------|
| `bug:` | silent-failure-hunter, code-reviewer |
| `security:` | security-reviewer |
| `performance:` | performance-reviewer |
| `breaking:` | code-reviewer (BC changes) |
| `arch:` | type-design-analyzer (design violations) |
| `type:` | type-design-analyzer (typing) |
| `test:` | pr-test-analyzer |
| `docs:` | comment-analyzer |
| `simplify:` | code-simplifier |

## What to do

1. Update the agent-spawn prompt template (SKILL.md, "Spawning Agent Reviewers" section, around L674) to require findings start with `<category>: ` followed by a 1-2 line issue+impact statement.
2. Each native subagent definition (per oma + bfd + 9ao) should declare its default category in its frontmatter or body, so the prompt template doesn't have to repeat the mapping.
3. User-authored agents in AGENT-REVIEWERS.md can declare their own category prefix; if not declared, default to `note:`.
4. Telemetry (per gx4) captures the category for each finding — useful for grouping/sorting in summaries.

## Why a spike

We don't know yet whether the prescriptive format helps or hurts. Plausible upsides:
- Easier to skim a PR with 20 inline comments
- Categorization signals to the PR author what kind of attention each comment needs (security-critical vs. style hint)
- Aligns with downstream tooling that wants to sort by severity/type

Plausible downsides:
- Agents may twist findings to fit a category rather than describe them naturally
- 1-2 line constraint may make complex findings underspecified
- The categories may not cleanly map to multi-faceted issues (a bug AND a performance issue)

Real-world usage will tell. Build it to measure.

## Interaction with other tickets

- **rb7 (confidence threshold + touch-it-you-own-it)** — finding stays the same, just gets a prefix
- **0nl (brainstorm-then-narrow)** — applies BEFORE the agent formats the finding for output
- **ydy (independent validator)** — validator examines the issue text post-prefix; should not depend on the prefix
- **77j (pending-review batching)** — comment bodies in the batched review use this format
- **vk0 (permalinks)** — permalink appears after the category line, on its own line

## Acceptance

- Spawn prompt template updated with the category prefix requirement and the mapping
- Per-agent default category captured in subagent frontmatter
- Telemetry captures the category as a structured field
- Configurable: when disabled, agents emit prose findings as before

## Combine with severity (from comprehensive-pr-reviewer review)

The category prefix (`bug:`, `security:`, ...) describes WHAT the finding is. udecode's reviewing-pr skill (re-published as comprehensive-pr-reviewer; see bd memory `provenance-comprehensive-pr-reviewer`) adds an orthogonal SEVERITY dimension via emoji prefix:

- 🚨 Critical — security holes, data loss, breaking API, crashes, exposed secrets
- ⚠️ Major — performance, logic errors, missing error handling, arch violations, BC issues
- 📝 Minor — style, naming, type annotations, suboptimal code
- 💡 Suggestions — refactors, alternatives, future work, nice-to-haves

Combined format: `<severity-emoji> <category>: <issue>` — example:

```
🚨 security: SQL injection via string concatenation — use parameterized query
⚠️ performance: N+1 query — fetch all items in a single call
📝 type: returns `any` for value that's known to be User | null
💡 simplify: nested ternary could be a switch statement
```

This combines the skim-ability of the category prefix with the at-a-glance severity from emoji. Both dimensions are useful for sorting/filtering downstream (gx4 telemetry, batched-review groupings in 77j).

Spike framing: implement as part of the same configurable format flag — when enabled, agents emit `<emoji> <category>:`; when disabled, agents emit prose. Telemetry captures both severity and category as structured fields.

title: pr-review-loop: replay test harness for offline A/B comparison of filter configs
description:
Build a test harness that can replay historical PR reviews through the pr-review-loop with different filter configurations, so we can A/B competing approaches offline against the same input rather than waiting for live usage.

## Why this is needed

Every ticket across the 4 learning epics (gnp, 37s, 0cp, 4fw) and gx4 is a spike — see bd memory `pr-review-loop-spike-framing`. Real-world usage will tell us which experiments earn their cost. But real-world data accumulates slowly, requires varied PR types, and is hard to compare apples-to-apples because each PR is different.

A replay harness solves this: fix the input (a recorded PR + its diff + the reviewers' actual feedback), vary the filter config, observe which configuration would have surfaced the issues that mattered.

## What to build

### 1. PR corpus capture

When the pr-review-loop runs, record everything needed to replay:
- PR metadata (title, description, base/head SHA, changed files)
- The full diff at the time of review
- Every finding emitted by every agent (raw, pre-filter)
- Every filter decision (which filter, what it said)
- Every comment posted, every reply, every commit pushed in response
- Final outcome per finding (Fixed / Won't fix / Out of scope / Unresolved)
