Format: per-PR directory under .beads/pr-review-loop/corpus/<repo>/<pr>/ with bundle.tar.gz containing diff + metadata + telemetry JSONL.

### 2. Replay engine

scripts/replay-pr.sh <corpus-bundle> [--config <yaml>]:

- Loads the recorded diff and PR metadata
- Re-spawns agents (with possibly different filter config) against the SAME input
- Does NOT post to GitHub; outputs would-be-posted findings to stdout/JSONL
- Records which findings the new config would have surfaced vs. dropped

### 3. Comparison report

scripts/compare-configs.sh <corpus-dir> <config-a.yaml> <config-b.yaml>:

- Replays the whole corpus under both configs
- Outputs:
  - Findings surfaced by A but not B (per agent, per severity)
  - Findings surfaced by B but not A
  - Findings both miss (where the ORIGINAL run caught a real issue that both configs would drop) — DANGER zone, investigate
  - Estimated token cost delta between A and B

### 4. Ground-truth dataset

To make replay useful, we need a labelled corpus: which findings were "real" (got Fixed) vs. "noise" (got Won't fix). The telemetry from gx4 already captures this; just need it persisted long enough to build the corpus.

Initial target: 20-50 PRs across varying sizes and languages, ideally including some intentionally-buggy seed PRs to test specific filter behaviors.

## Acceptance

- Corpus capture lives alongside normal loop operation (opt-in, off by default for storage reasons)
- replay-pr.sh can reproduce a recorded review under a new config without hitting GitHub
- compare-configs.sh produces a side-by-side delta for 2 configs across an N-PR corpus
- At least one of the filter spike tickets (rb7, ydy, 0nl) has data from the harness informing its keep/drop decision

## Open questions

- Storage size of bundle.tar.gz per PR (diffs can be big; need compression strategy)
- Privacy: does the corpus belong in the repo, in a separate private repo, or local-only? Probably local-only with explicit opt-in to share.
- Reproducibility: agents are non-deterministic. May need to record multiple runs per config to get a reliable signal, OR seed prompts in a way that improves determinism.

## Relationship to other tickets

- **gx4** (filter framework + telemetry) — provides the per-finding JSONL this harness ingests. gx4 should land first.
- **rb7 / ydy / 0nl** — the harness is the right way to decide which of these to keep, drop, or stack.
- All other tickets across the 4 learning epics — once the harness exists, any future spike can be validated against a fixed corpus before committing.

## Timeline expectation

Don't build this until gx4 is in place AND at least one filter (rb7) has been collecting telemetry for a few weeks. Premature otherwise — no data to drive design choices.

title: pr-review-loop: sensitive-area escalation gate (DB schema, auth, API, infra, hot paths)
description:
Add a pre-flight (or in-loop) detector that escalates to the user when the PR touches code areas that aren't safe to auto-iterate on.

## Why

Our autonomous loop is designed for review-and-fix cycles where the agent and the human can both reason about the change. But some categories of change require human judgment that the loop can't substitute for. Sontek's Code Review Expert SKILL.md lists these as "Long-Term Considerations — flag for senior engineer review":

- Database schema modifications or migrations
- API contract changes (REST, GraphQL, gRPC)
- Authentication/authorization logic changes
- New framework or library adoption
- Performance-critical code paths (hot paths)
- Security-sensitive functionality
- Infrastructure or deployment changes
- Significant architectural decisions

For our loop, when a PR touches any of these, the right behavior isn't "auto-iterate on bot feedback" but "pause and let a human drive."

## What to build

1. **Detector** scripts/sensitive-area-check.sh that inspects the PR diff (file list, optional content patterns) and reports which sensitive categories the PR touches. Categories detected by heuristic:
   - DB migrations: paths matching */migrations/* or alembic/versions/*, *.sql in known migration dirs
   - API contracts: openapi.yaml / *.proto / *.graphql / api/v*.* or marked controllers
   - Auth: paths matching auth/, identity/, sessions/, login/, *jwt*, *oauth*
   - Infra: terraform/, Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml, .github/workflows/, kustomize/, k8s/
   - New deps: package.json/go.mod/Cargo.toml/requirements.txt/pyproject.toml diff shows added dependencies
   - Heuristic for perf hot paths and architectural decisions: needs LLM judgement — punt to a haiku Task

2. **Loop hook** — call sensitive-area-check.sh at loop start. If any category fires:
   - REPORT to the user which categories matched and which files
   - ASK whether to:
     (a) PROCEED with normal autonomous loop (user accepts the risk)
     (b) RUN A SINGLE ROUND in advisory mode (agents review and emit findings, but no auto-fix; user drives all fixes)
     (c) STOP — defer this PR for manual review
   - DEFAULT: do not auto-pick. The user must answer.

3. **Optional --skip-sensitive-check flag** for users who want unconditional loop behavior.

## Interaction with v7h (pre-flight skip)

- v7h checks if the PR should be reviewed at all (closed/draft/trivial)
- This ticket checks if the PR should be reviewed AUTONOMOUSLY
- Order: v7h runs first (cheap exit), then this check (uses the result if proceeding)

## Acceptance

- Detector script exists, identifies sensitive categories with reasonable precision
- Loop short-circuits with a 3-way question when sensitive areas are touched
- --skip-sensitive-check bypass exists
- Touch-it-you-own-it still applies (rb7) when the loop proceeds — sensitive doesn't mean skip-pre-existing

title: pr-review-loop: brainstorm-then-narrow (5-7 → 1-2) investigation pattern in spawn prompt
description:
Add a "brainstorm-then-narrow" structuring step to the agent-spawn prompt template (SKILL.md, "Spawning Agent Reviewers" section, around L674) so agents broaden their consideration before committing to findings.

## The pattern

From sontek's Code Review Expert SKILL.md (https://mcpmarket.com/tools/skills/code-review-expert-8):

> ## Investigation Approach
>
> When reviewing code:
>
> - List 5-7 potential issues or concerns for each file
> - Gather evidence (check similar patterns, run tests, trace data flow)
> - Narrow to 1-2 most critical issues per file
> - Verify issues are real (not false positives or already handled)
> - Only report confirmed, actionable feedback

## Why this differs from what's already in our queue

- **rb7 (confidence threshold)** filters individual findings AFTER the agent thought of them. Doesn't change how the agent generates candidates.
- **ydy (independent validator)** runs AFTER findings are emitted. Doesn't affect agent reasoning.
- This pattern shapes WHAT the agent considers in the first place: broad first, then narrow. Forces the agent to think laterally before locking in.

Useful in particular for the security-reviewer and performance-reviewer agents (devon-claude-skills-<secperf-ticket>) where there are many things to check and the agent might focus on the first issue it spots and miss broader ones.

## What to do

Add this section to the agent-spawn prompt template (single instruction block, applies to all spawned agents):

> ## Investigation approach
>
> For each file in your scope:
> 1. Brainstorm 5-7 potential concerns spanning your focus area
> 2. For each candidate, gather evidence: check similar patterns in the codebase, look at related tests, trace data flow
> 3. Narrow to the 1-2 most credible/critical concerns
> 4. For each survivor, confirm it's a real issue (not already handled, not a false positive, not pre-existing-and-out-of-scope)
> 5. Apply the confidence threshold (rb7) and touch-it-you-own-it scope filter (rb7) before posting

## Interaction with other filters

This stage runs INSIDE the agent. It's complementary to:
- The confidence rubric and do-not-flag rules from rb7 (applied within the brainstorm → narrow → confirm flow)
- The two-stage validator from ydy (runs AFTER posting; catches whatever survived this filter)

Three-layer filter: broad-then-narrow (agent) → confidence threshold (agent) → independent validation (external subagent). Catches different failure modes at each layer.

## Acceptance

- Spawn prompt template in SKILL.md includes the brainstorm-then-narrow section
- Agent-reviewer outputs show evidence of brainstorming (in their internal reasoning if logged)
- Per-round finding counts trend down without losing critical-severity findings

## Treat this as a configurable hypothesis, not baked-in policy

Per devon-claude-skills-gx4, this filter must be:
- Toggleable via config (on/off)
- Parameter-tunable (threshold value / brainstorm counts / validator model selection, as applicable)
- Telemetry-emitting per the JSONL schema in that ticket

We don't know yet whether this filter earns its cost in which combinations with the other two. Real-world usage will tell us. Implement it to *measure*, not to assert.

title: pr-review-loop: investigate mcp__github_inline_comment as replacement for gh api scripts
description:
Boris Cherny's code-review plugin uses `mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true` to post inline review comments, instead of raw `gh api` calls. Investigate whether this MCP server is usable for our scripts.

## Why investigate

Our scripts (post-line-comment.sh, reply-to-comment.sh, etc.) shell out to `gh api`. That works but has costs:
- Hard-to-handle JSON in bash (we escape via jq/sed)
- Each script reimplements quoting and `-f` vs `-F` gotchas
- No structured error reporting; failures surface as raw HTTP/JSON
- Different scripts for different comment types (PR comment, line comment, reply)

A first-party MCP server would be a single typed tool that the model can call directly with structured args. Reduced script surface, fewer escape bugs.

## What to check

1. Is `mcp__github_inline_comment` Anthropic-internal-only, or available via a public MCP server? Check:
   - Claude Code docs at https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code
   - Anthropic MCP server repos
   - Their plugin's command frontmatter declares it under `allowed-tools` — implies it's configured at the harness level, not user-installable

2. If available externally, what does the tool signature look like? (path, line, body, confirmed flag — what else?)

3. Does it support batching (multiple inline comments in one call)? If yes, this could supersede devon-claude-skills-77j's pending-review approach with something simpler.

4. Does it handle suggestion blocks correctly (devon-claude-skills-08d)?

5. Cost: does using the MCP tool count differently than `gh api` (no — both are just tool calls), or is there a quota/auth difference?

## Outcome

Either:
- **Available + works for us**: file a follow-up to replace the `gh api`-based scripts with MCP calls. May reduce or replace 77j entirely.
- **Internal-only or doesn't fit**: document the finding in the audit so we don't re-investigate, close this ticket.

## Reference

anthropics/claude-code plugins/code-review/commands/code-review.md:
- frontmatter: `allowed-tools: ... mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment`
- step 9: `Post inline comments for each issue using mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment with confirmed: true`
close_reason: Won't do — sticking with gh CLI. MCP-based inline-comment tool wastes tokens vs the gh api scripts we already have. The pending-review batching approach in devon-claude-skills-77j (gh api /pulls/<PR>/reviews) is the chosen path. Decision recorded: gh stays.

title: pr-review-loop: emit GitHub permalinks (full SHA + line context) in agent comments
description:
When agent reviewers post comments, include GitHub permalinks to the exact code being commented on, using the format that renders correctly in inline-comment markdown.

## Why

Boris Cherny's code-review plugin requires every issue to link the code with:
- Full git SHA (NOT abbreviated, NOT `$(git rev-parse HEAD)`)
- `/blob/<sha>/<path>` URL form
- `#Lstart-Lend` line range
- At least 1 line of context BEFORE and AFTER the commented line

Example: `https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/c21d3c10bc8e898b7ac1a2d745bdc9bc4e423afe/package.json#L10-L15`

If you commented about line 5-6, the link should be L4-L7 (one line of context on each side).

This makes inline comments self-contained: the reader sees the issue body AND clicks through to a properly-anchored view of the code.

Our current scripts/post-line-comment.sh emits no such link — it relies on GitHub's own line-anchor for the inline comment, which works in the GitHub UI but is lost if the comment text is read out of context (in a summary, in our beads tickets for out-of-scope findings, in cross-PR references).

## What to build

1. Update scripts/post-line-comment.sh to compute the GitHub permalink and append it to the comment body (after the issue text, before the agent signature).
2. Update the agent-spawn prompt template (SKILL.md, around L674) to instruct agents to ALWAYS include the permalink when proposing a finding — they have the file/line so they can construct it.
3. Helper utility scripts/permalink.sh `<file>` `<start>` `<end>` that outputs the properly-formatted URL given the current HEAD SHA. Agents call this once per finding.

## Interaction with other tickets

- **77j** (pending review batching) — permalinks travel inside the batched review comment bodies. Same surface area.
- **08d** (suggestion blocks) — orthogonal but both improve agent comment quality.
- **q2h** (beads tickets for out-of-scope) — permalinks captured in beads tickets give us a stable reference even after the PR is merged.

## Acceptance

- Permalinks present in every agent-posted line comment
- Format validated against the rules above (full SHA, +/-1 line context, /blob/ form)
- Beads tickets for out-of-scope findings include permalinks too

title: pr-review-loop: model-tiered agent dispatch (haiku/sonnet/opus by task type)
description:
When spawning agent reviewers (and other supporting subagents in the loop), select model by focus area rather than hardcoding sonnet for all.

## Current behavior

The agent-spawn prompt template in SKILL.md (Spawning Agent Reviewers section, ~L674) hardcodes `model: sonnet` for every agent. This is wasteful for cheap pre-flight checks and underpowered for hard bug-detection work.

## Reference pattern

anthropics/claude-code code-review plugin dispatches by task:
- haiku → cheap pre-flight (is PR draft/trivial/etc), CLAUDE.md path discovery
- sonnet → CLAUDE.md compliance checks, validation of CLAUDE.md findings
- opus → bug detection, validation of bug findings

## What to do

1. **Update default model per agent type** in the 6 native subagent definitions we ship via devon-claude-skills-bfd:
   - code-reviewer (CLAUDE.md compliance) → sonnet
   - comment-analyzer → sonnet
   - silent-failure-hunter (bug-class) → opus
   - pr-test-analyzer (logic) → opus
   - type-design-analyzer → sonnet
   - code-simplifier → opus (reasoning-heavy refactor)

2. **Update supporting scripts/tasks** in the loop:
   - Pre-flight check (devon-claude-skills-...): haiku
   - Finding-validator subagents (devon-claude-skills-...): opus for bug, sonnet for CLAUDE.md
   - Summarize-reviews helper if invoked from Task: haiku
   - CI fix Task: sonnet (default), opus if bug looks structural

3. **Document the convention** in SKILL.md so user-authored AGENT-REVIEWERS.md entries get a recommendation: "if your agent looks for hard bugs / security issues, prefer opus; if it checks style/compliance, prefer sonnet."

## Dependencies

- Easier after devon-claude-skills-oma (native subagent system) is in place, because per-agent `model:` lives in subagent frontmatter
- Coordinates with devon-claude-skills-bfd (the 6 defaults) — the model choice is baked into each shipped subagent

## Acceptance

- Each of the 6 default subagents has an explicit model: line matching its focus area
- SKILL.md documents the rationale and gives user authors a guideline
- Loop logs show distinct model usage per agent


---

## STATUS UPDATE (2026-05-15): partly shipped, partly distributed across other tickets

This ticket is now effectively a TRACKING umbrella; the work is distributed:

| Component | Status | Where |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Per-default model selection on the 6 native specialists | **SHIPPED via bfd** (commit 0000990) | All 6 default subagents at `plugins/pr-review-loop/agents/*.md` carry the right `model:` frontmatter |
| Spawn template uses per-agent model field | **SHIPPED via bfd** | Documented in SKILL.md "Spawning Agent Reviewers" |
| Pre-flight check uses haiku | **Pending — covered by devon-claude-skills-v7h** | When v7h is built, the pre-flight Task uses haiku per its acceptance criteria |
| Finding-validator subagents tiered (opus for bug-class, sonnet for compliance) | **Pending — covered by devon-claude-skills-ydy** | Folded into ydy's acceptance criteria as of 2026-05-15 |
| Summarize-reviews helper Task uses haiku | **Pending — minor optimization** | Could be done as a small focused PR alongside any larger work; not blocking |
| CI fix Task model selection | **Pending — minor optimization** | Default sonnet; opus for structural bugs. Best done when working CI fix loop next |
| Document the convention in SKILL.md | **Pending — small standalone doc add** | Recommend opus for bug/security agents, sonnet for style/compliance agents in user-authored AGENT-REVIEWERS.md entries |

## Closing this ticket

Recommend closing 0np when:
- v7h ships with haiku for pre-flight (handles row 3)
- ydy ships with tiered validators (handles row 4)
- A small SKILL.md doc-add ships (handles row 7)

Rows 5 and 6 are minor; can be deferred indefinitely or rolled into other PRs opportunistically.

title: pr-review-loop: pre-flight skip check for draft/closed/trivial PRs
description:
Add a cheap pre-flight check at loop start that skips obviously-unreviewable PRs (closed, draft, trivial/automated, etc.) without doing the work of a full round.

## Why

Our loop currently enters its first round unconditionally. If the PR is a draft, closed, a dependabot bump, or a trivial whitespace change, we'll still spawn agents and trigger reviews — wasted tokens and CI quota.

Boris Cherny's code-review plugin starts with a haiku agent that checks four conditions and stops if any are true:
- PR is closed
- PR is draft
- PR doesn't need review (automated PR, trivial change obviously correct)
- Claude has already commented on this PR

We want the same guard, with one modification: **do NOT skip when Claude has already commented**, since for our autonomous loop, prior Claude activity is the point. So our skip conditions are just the first three.

## What to build

1. A new script scripts/preflight-check.sh that runs a haiku Task with the PR state and returns one of:
   - `proceed` (no skip condition matched)
   - `skip:closed`
   - `skip:draft`
   - `skip:trivial`

2. Call it at the very start of the loop (before round 1). If output starts with `skip:`, report the reason to the user and exit.

3. Add an override flag (e.g. `--force` on whatever loop entry the user uses) so a deliberate run on a draft PR still works.

## What counts as "trivial"

Leave it to haiku's judgment with a prompt like:
> A PR is trivial if it is an automated dependency bump, version-only change, generated file update, whitespace-only change, comment-only fix, or a single-line typo correction. Anything that introduces new logic, modifies behavior, or touches test/source files non-trivially is NOT trivial.

## Reference

anthropics/claude-code plugins/code-review/commands/code-review.md step 1.

## Acceptance

- Loop short-circuits with a clear reason on draft/closed/trivial PRs
- `--force` flag bypasses the check
- Trivial-detection prompt is conservative (false-negative bias — better to review a not-quite-trivial PR than skip a real one)

## Sequenced with deterministic pre-checks (from MiniMax-AI review)

devon-claude-skills-dl6 introduces deterministic pre-checks (secret scan, linter, type-check) that run before round 1. The relationship to this ticket (v7h pre-flight skip):

- **v7h runs first** (cheap exit) — decides *whether* to review at all (draft/closed/trivial → bail)
- **dl6 runs second** (if v7h passes) — decides *what to fix first* deterministically before spawning agents

Both are pre-loop guards but at different layers: should-we-review (v7h) → what-deterministic-issues-block-review (dl6) → round 1 agent spawn.

In the loop entry sequence:

1. v7h pre-flight skip check (haiku-class: draft/closed/trivial)
2. dl6 deterministic pre-checks (secret scan + lint + typecheck)
3. vsf sensitive-area gate (decides autonomous vs advisory mode)
4. Round 1 agent spawning begins

title: devon-claude-skills: add CHANGELOG.md and per-plugin SemVer discipline
description:
We have no top-level CHANGELOG and no per-plugin version bumps tracked anywhere visible. Each plugin change is currently invisible to consumers reading the repo.

## What to do

1. Add CHANGELOG.md at repo root following Keep a Changelog format (Added/Changed/Deprecated/Removed/Fixed/Security sections, Unreleased at top, dated versioned sections below)
2. Decide on versioning unit:
   - Option A: one repo-level version covering all plugins (simpler, less precise)
   - Option B: per-plugin versions tracked in each plugin's .claude-plugin/plugin.json (when we add it — see devon-claude-skills-oma) and a per-plugin CHANGELOG, with the repo CHANGELOG cross-referencing them
3. Backfill recent significant changes from git log (last ~10 commits worth) into the initial CHANGELOG
4. Document the update process in CONTRIBUTING or README (move Unreleased items into dated version on release, tag the commit)

## Why

- Marketplace consumers can read what changed between versions before upgrading
- Helps us remember what we shipped when looking back
- Standard practice for any distributable plugin

## Reference

aidankinzett/claude-git-pr-skill has a clean example: https://github.com/aidankinzett/claude-git-pr-skill/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md — they keep an Unreleased section at the top and add Planned items in advance.

## Acceptance

- CHANGELOG.md exists at repo root with at least one backfilled version section
- Process for updating it on release is documented somewhere a future contributor will find
- Decision on per-plugin vs repo-level versioning recorded as a beads decision (bd decision)

title: pr-review-loop: emit GitHub suggestion blocks in agent reviewer findings
description:
Have agent reviewers include GitHub-renderable suggestion blocks in their line comments when they can propose a concrete code change.

## Why

GitHub renders triple-backtick `suggestion` blocks with a one-click "Commit suggestion" button. For our loop's typical case (Claude reviews and fixes its own PR), Claude usually applies the fix directly so this is low value. But:

- When pr-review-loop runs on a human-authored PR (rare but possible)
- When findings are deferred to follow-up (out of scope → beads ticket, but the suggestion is still useful in the comment for context)
- When the human reviewer reads the agent's output and wants to one-click apply

...the suggestion block adds real value.

## What to do

Update the agent-spawn prompt template (SKILL.md, "Spawning Agent Reviewers" section, around L674) to encourage but not require suggestion blocks:

- When the agent has a concrete code replacement for a single line or contiguous range, emit a fenced block tagged `suggestion` containing the replacement code.
- When the suggestion would target a markdown file containing triple backticks, use 4 backticks or tildes (edge case from reference skill).
- When the fix is conceptual or spans non-contiguous lines, fall back to prose.

## Reference

aidankinzett/claude-git-pr-skill SKILL.md has good gotchas for nested backticks and the syntax rules.

## Dependencies

Better adopted alongside devon-claude-skills-77j (the pending-review batching ticket), since the suggestion bodies travel inside the batched review comments. Independent though — could land first.
## Additional refinement (from anthropics/claude-code code-review plugin review)

When emitting suggestion blocks, follow Boris Cherny's rule:

> Never post a committable suggestion UNLESS committing the suggestion fixes the issue ENTIRELY. If follow-up steps are required, do not leave a committable suggestion — describe the issue and proposed fix in prose instead.

Also from the same source:

> For larger fixes (6+ lines, structural changes, or changes spanning multiple locations), describe the issue and suggested fix WITHOUT a suggestion block.

These two rules should be in the spawn prompt template alongside the suggestion-block syntax guidance.

title: pr-review-loop: concrete 'When to invoke' scenarios in agent-spawn prompt
description:
The upstream pr-review-toolkit agent definitions include 2-3 worked invocation scenarios ('Fresh PR, thoroughness check'; 'PR updated with new logic'; 'Pre-PR sanity check'). These improve trigger accuracy by giving the agent concrete patterns to match.

## What to do

Update our generic agent-spawn prompt template in SKILL.md ('Spawning Agent Reviewers' section, around L674) to include a 'When to invoke' style framing that the orchestrator fills in based on round context:

- First round on a fresh PR
- Mid-loop after fixes
- Final-verification round

Include short example expectations for each, so the agent calibrates its threshold (more aggressive on round 1, more conservative on final verification).

## Acceptance

- Spawn prompt template updated in SKILL.md
- A spot check of one agent reviewer run shows the agent referencing the round context in its review

title: pr-review-loop: structured Critical/Important/Suggestions output in round + merge summary
description:
Adopt the upstream pr-review-toolkit's structured output framing (Critical / Important / Suggestions / Strengths) for the merge-readiness summary and per-round aggregation.

## Why

Our current merge-readiness summary (SKILL.md ~L224) is process-metric heavy: 'rounds completed, comments addressed, fixed/won't-fix counts'. That tells the human what the loop *did* but not what was *found*. The toolkit's command renders an aggregated:

- Critical Issues (must fix before merge)
- Important Issues (should fix)
- Suggestions (nice to have)
- Positive Observations / Strengths

This is more useful to whoever reads the summary at end of loop.

## What to do

1. Update the merge-readiness summary section in SKILL.md to include Critical/Important/Suggestions/Strengths buckets, populated from the reviewer findings of the loop (not just process counters)
2. Consider doing the same after each round if it does not get noisy
3. Make sure 'won't fix' and 'out of scope' findings still appear (separately) so they don't get lost

## Use sontek's detailed output template as the source structure

Sontek's Code Review Expert SKILL.md (https://mcpmarket.com/tools/skills/code-review-expert-8) provides a complete output template that's more detailed than the simple Critical/Important/Suggestions framing from pr-review-toolkit:

```markdown
## Code Review Summary

**Overall Assessment:** [Approve/Request Changes/Comment]

**Key Changes:** [Brief summary of what the PR does]

## Critical Issues

[List blocking issues if any]

## Important Issues

[List significant issues that should be addressed]

## Suggestions

[List nice-to-have improvements]

## Positive Feedback

[Call out good practices, clever solutions, or improvements]

## Testing

[Comment on test coverage and quality]

## Recommendation

[Final recommendation with any conditions]
```

Adapt for our merge-readiness summary at end of the loop:

- **Overall Assessment** → replace with our existing "loop status" (converged / paused / blocked-on-CI / escalated)
- **Key Changes** → keep (PR description summary)
- **Critical/Important/Suggestions** → keep, populated from reviewer findings
- **Positive Feedback** → keep (rare but worth recognising; agents should track these)
- **Testing** → keep (pr-test-analyzer feeds this section directly)
- **Recommendation** → keep, framed around merge readiness (per existing SKILL.md merge-readiness section)

Also add: section listing beads tickets created during the loop for out-of-scope findings, and section listing any sensitive-area escalations (devon-claude-skills-vsf) flagged but proceeded-through.

title: pr-review-loop: optional code-simplifier polish round before merge
description:
Run a single code-simplifier pass after the review loop converges but before merge-readiness check, to catch unnecessary complexity that pure correctness reviewers miss.

## Why

Our loop exits when reviewers stop producing actionable feedback. That filters for correctness but not for clarity — code can be correct yet needlessly complex (nested ternaries, dense one-liners, redundant abstractions added during fixes). The upstream pr-review-toolkit explicitly recommends running code-simplifier *after* review passes; we should adopt the same shape.

## What to do

1. After the 'final verification round' produces no actionable feedback, run code-simplifier (one of the 6 defaults — see sibling ticket) as a one-shot
2. If it returns simplifications worth applying, apply them, commit, push, and run ONE more verification round
3. If nothing actionable, proceed to merge-readiness

## Notes

- Make this opt-out, default-on; some users will not want stylistic changes touching their PR late
- Should NOT touch code if reviewers already explicitly approved the current state (e.g. 'looks good, do not change')

title: pr-review-loop: confidence threshold (>=80/100) in agent spawn prompt
description:
Adopt the upstream code-reviewer confidence-scoring pattern in our generic agent-spawn prompt so agent reviewers self-filter low-signal findings before posting.

## Current behavior

The agent-spawn prompt in SKILL.md (around L674 — the 'Spawning Agent Reviewers' section) says 'Be thorough but not pedantic' but provides no quantitative threshold. Agents post borderline findings that get triaged as 'Won't fix', wasting round time.

## Proposed change

Add a confidence rubric to the spawn prompt:

> Rate each finding 0-100:
> - 0-25: likely false positive
> - 26-50: minor nitpick not in CLAUDE.md
> - 51-75: valid but low-impact
> - 76-90: important
> - 91-100: critical bug or explicit guideline violation
>
> Only post findings with confidence >= 80.

## Touch-it-you-own-it: in-scope rule for touched files

**Our review philosophy diverges from Boris Cherny's upstream:** when you touch code, you become responsible for it. The PR is the opportunity to make the code better, not just to avoid making it worse.

This applies to BOTH:

- **Pre-existing bugs** in files modified by this PR
- **General quality gaps** in those same files: missing/weak test coverage for code paths the PR exercises, vague-but-real security concerns, weak input validation, missing error handling, etc.

If the PR exercises or exposes a quality gap in touched code, the gap is in scope — regardless of whether it pre-existed or whether CLAUDE.md explicitly names it.

Triage flow for these findings:

- **Trivial fix** (one-liner, localized, no behavior change beyond the fix) → fix it as part of this PR
- **Non-trivial fix** (broader scope, design change, risky, requires its own thinking) → out of scope for this PR → file a beads ticket via the existing out-of-scope flow (SKILL.md "Out of Scope Suggestions"), reply "Out of scope - tracked in BD-XXX"

Issues in UNTOUCHED files (files not appearing in this PR's diff) remain genuinely out of scope and should not be flagged — that's a different PR's job.

The spawn prompt should make the "touched vs untouched" distinction explicit: agents already receive their scoped `changed_files` list, so this is a natural filter.

## Do NOT flag

(Adapted from anthropics/claude-code code-review plugin, with both the pre-existing-issues item and the general-quality item REMOVED per the touch-it-you-own-it rule above)

- Code that LOOKS like a bug but is actually correct
- Pedantic nitpicks a senior engineer would skip
- Issues a linter will catch (and do NOT run the linter to verify — saves a tool call)
- Issues mentioned in CLAUDE.md but explicitly silenced in the code (lint-ignore comments etc.) — someone made an explicit decision; respect it
- Issues in files outside the PR's changed_files scope

What's NOT on this list: pre-existing bugs in touched files, weak test coverage of touched code paths, vague-but-real security or validation gaps in touched code. Those go through the triage flow above.

## Only flag (HIGH SIGNAL)

- Code that won't compile or parse (syntax/type errors, missing imports, unresolved references)
- Code that will definitely produce wrong results regardless of inputs (clear logic errors)
- **CLAUDE.md violations where you can quote the exact rule being broken** — forcing function that prevents "violates the spirit of X"
- Pre-existing bugs and quality gaps in touched files (per the triage flow above)

If you are not certain an issue is real, do not flag it. False positives erode trust.

## Acceptance

- Spawn prompt template in SKILL.md updated with the confidence rubric, the touched-vs-untouched distinction, the do-not-flag list, and the high-signal criteria
- "Pre-existing in touched files" triage flow is clearly documented and references the existing out-of-scope/beads workflow
- See devon-claude-skills-ydy for the structurally stronger validation-pass approach that complements this in-agent filter

## Treat this as a configurable hypothesis, not baked-in policy

Per devon-claude-skills-gx4, this filter must be:
- Toggleable via config (on/off)
- Parameter-tunable (threshold value / brainstorm counts / validator model selection, as applicable)
- Telemetry-emitting per the JSONL schema in that ticket

We don't know yet whether this filter earns its cost in which combinations with the other two. Real-world usage will tell us. Implement it to *measure*, not to assert.

title: SKILL.md A.4: add 'do not narrate per-frame' guidance for sub-Task callers
description:
During the 15-video batch run, one Task (AIFi8OnN5oU) ran out of output budget mid-classification because it narrated each frame's decision inline before saving state to disk. The retry succeeded after explicit instructions: 'do NOT narrate per-frame classifications; save state via Write tool after each batch; keep running text under 50 words per batch.'

Phase A.4 in SKILL.md tells the agent to spawn sub-Tasks with the kind taxonomy and value-filter inline. It should also tell those sub-Tasks (and the main agent) to:
- Append cumulative classifications to <work>/classifications.json after each batch via Write tool
- Keep narrative output minimal (one-liner per batch is fine)
- Don't dump the full classification dict for each frame in the response — that's what the JSON file is for

Same guidance applies to A.7 audit when run as a sub-Task.

Acceptance: SKILL.md A.4 has an explicit 'output discipline' note. A future agent running A.4 against a 100+ candidate video doesn't need a retry.

title: Promote /tmp/yt-batch helper scripts into youtube-synthesizer scripts/
description:
The 15-video batch run on 2026-04-29 relied on three helper scripts living in /tmp/yt-batch/ rather than in the canonical skill scripts/ dir:

- /tmp/yt-batch/build_entry.py — composes Phase B/C/D from work-dir state (metadata.json + transcript.txt + keep_list_final.json + c.json), copies frames as image-NN.png, writes the .md
- /tmp/yt-batch/dedup.py — Phase A.6 cross-time pHash dedup helper (excludes animated-build-step)
- /tmp/yt-batch/scan_motion.py — Phase 1.7 motion-bisection scanner (the version used to add motion grids to the ox-on-river entry)

These should move to plugins/youtube-synthesizer/skills/youtube-synthesizer/scripts/ as canonical primitives, with SKILL.md updated to invoke them by their stable paths instead of the /tmp ad-hoc copies.

scan_motion.py may need generalization first — its current shape was tailored to the ox-rerun e2e. Worth a separate review pass to extract the reusable parts.

Acceptance: a fresh agent can run the synthesizer end-to-end without referencing anything under /tmp.

title: pr-review-loop: cross-provider validator support (Gemini, OpenAI, local) as follow-on to qml
description:
## Why

Follow-on to devon-claude-skills-qml (per-agent validator_model override). Once users can choose a specific Anthropic model per agent, the natural next step is choosing a non-Anthropic validator — Gemini, OpenAI, a local Llama, etc.

Use cases:
- **Independence by construction**: validating an opus-flagged finding with Gemini Pro gives a genuinely cross-model second look, not just opus-checking-opus
- **Cost**: a cheap open-source local model (Llama, Qwen) for high-volume P3-style validators
- **Provider trust**: organizations standardizing on one provider can validate findings against another to avoid single-provider blind spots
- **Hybrid budgets**: spend opus tokens on flagging, cheaper non-Anthropic models on validation

## Depends on

devon-claude-skills-qml — the per-agent validator config surface must land first. This ticket adds provider plurality on top of that surface.

## Design sketch

### Config shape

In 'AGENT-REVIEWERS.md', extend the validator config from a single 'validator_model' field to a two-field shape:

```markdown
## security-reviewer

validator_provider: gemini
validator_model: gemini-2.5-pro

[reviewer body...]
```

Default 'validator_provider: anthropic' when only 'validator_model' is set (preserves qml's behavior).

### Invocation

For 'validator_provider: anthropic': use Task tool with the named model (qml's behavior).

For other providers: shell out to a provider-specific validator script. The skill ships one per supported provider:

- 'scripts/_validator-anthropic.sh' (default path; wraps Task)
- 'scripts/_validator-gemini.sh' (calls 'gemini' CLI or REST)
- 'scripts/_validator-openai.sh' (calls 'openai' CLI or REST)
- 'scripts/_validator-ollama.sh' (calls local 'ollama run' for self-hosted models)

Each script's contract:
- stdin: JSON {severity, location, body, diff_context}
- stdout: 'VERDICT: VALID | INVALID | UNCERTAIN' newline 'REASON: <one-line>'
- exit code: 0 on success, non-zero on auth/quota/network failure
- The loop treats exit-code failure as if validator returned UNCERTAIN (defensive: don't drop findings because Gemini's API is down)

### Auth

Each provider script picks up auth from its own env var family:
- anthropic: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (or Claude Code session)
- gemini: GEMINI_API_KEY
- openai: OPENAI_API_KEY
- ollama: OLLAMA_HOST (defaults to localhost:11434)

Skill prerequisites section documents the env-var requirement per provider.

### Telemetry

The qml ticket's caveat ('overridden agent's per-flagger acceptance rate is not directly comparable') extends naturally — now the comparison surface is (provider, model, flagger), not just (model, flagger). Telemetry output should include both fields when overridden.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] 'validator_provider' field parseable from AGENT-REVIEWERS.md (default 'anthropic')
- [ ] Provider scripts: 'scripts/_validator-anthropic.sh' (refactored from inline Task logic) + at least one non-Anthropic implementation (Gemini suggested as POC since gh + gemini-code-assist are already in this skill's orbit)
- [ ] Defensive on provider failure: exit-code != 0 → treat as UNCERTAIN (don't drop findings on network blip)
- [ ] Telemetry includes (provider, model) per finding outcome
- [ ] Prerequisites section documents env-var requirements per provider
- [ ] At least one end-to-end test using a non-Anthropic validator

## Out of scope

- Self-hosting infrastructure (Ollama setup, etc.) — only document that the script works against an existing setup
- Provider-by-provider parity testing — POC with one non-Anthropic provider is sufficient for first cut

pr-review-loop filter design principle: confidence-threshold (rb7), brainstorm-then-narrow (0nl), and independent-validator (ydy) are three competing hypotheses for filtering noisy findings — not a baked-in stack. Build them as configurable, toggleable, parameter-tunable. Emit per-finding telemetry so real-world usage can show which earns its cost in which combinations. Tracked in devon-claude-skills-gx4.

Image-read request size limit: reading too many full-res PNG frames in one tool call hits 'Request too large (max 32MB)'. Frames at 1280x720 PNG are ~500KB-1.5MB each; ~20 reads in a single message can blow the limit. Always batch image reads in groups of 2-3 when classifying or auditing the synthesizer's keep-list. Per-second thumbnails at 320x180 JPEG would be much smaller (~10-30KB each) but currently used only by discover.py internally.

pr-review-loop's unique strengths vs all 8 peer skills audited: (1) MOST AGENTIC by an order of magnitude — only skill that autonomously iterates rounds (push, wait, fetch, fix, reply, commit, push) without human gates until convergence; every peer is single-shot, human-initiated, human-actioned; (2) AGENT-REVIEWERS.md configuration system is uniquely flexible — hierarchical directory scoping, override-by-depth, per-agent state tracking, context-section inheritance — nobody else has anything close; closest peer (anthropics/pr-review-toolkit) hardcodes 6 agents with no per-repo customization. Real weaknesses: (a) ship zero default agents — users must author AGENT-REVIEWERS.md to benefit from the config system (bfd + 9ao fixes); (b) filter sophistication — 'be skeptical' in prose, no confidence threshold/validator/severity discipline (rb7+ydy+0nl+5zk roadmap). Plugin hygiene is fine at the collection-marketplace level (.claude-plugin/marketplace.json) — what looks missing is per-plugin polish (commands/, native agents/), not foundational scaffolding. Audited 2026-05-14 across 8 peer skills.

Code review philosophy (touch-it-you-own-it): when you touch code, you become responsible for it. In scope for review in TOUCHED files (files in the PR diff): pre-existing bugs AND general quality gaps (weak/missing tests for exercised paths, vague-but-real security or input-validation gaps, missing error handling). Triage: trivial → fix in the PR, non-trivial → beads ticket via out-of-scope flow. Issues in UNTOUCHED files remain out of scope. Diverges from Boris Cherny's upstream code-review plugin (which says skip pre-existing AND general quality). Captured in ticket devon-claude-skills-rb7.

youtube-synthesizer Phase A classification drift: when classifying 100+ candidate frames in batches of 10-15, per-frame attention drift causes some talking-head and transition frames to get mistakenly tagged as keep-kinds. Phase 1.6 e2e against MIqpvpNS5pI: 4 talking-head frames + 1 empty cartoon + 1 redundant build-up state slipped through (6/33 = 18% error rate). Mitigation: add a Phase A.7 whole-list audit pass that re-reads every kept frame as a unit and prunes the noise. The audit is a separate pass from A.4 classification; its job is to catch drift and curate.

youtube-synthesizer pHash dedup threshold (≤5 hamming) is too strict for visually-redundant frames. Phase 1.5 e2e against MIqpvpNS5pI: two opening map frames at t=0.0 and t=0.5 measured hamming=28 (different) despite being effectively the same fantasy map (slight pan/zoom). Both kept by strict rule. Visual judgment would dedup. Consider: agent-judged dedup pass after pHash, or a higher threshold (~15) for same-kind consecutive keepers. Tracked for future tuning.

youtube-synthesizer pHash thresholds (SAME_MAX=5, DIFFERENT_MIN=20) validated against the grain-haul test video (MIqpvpNS5pI): coarse sampling at 30-100s intervals produced 22 consecutive pairs, of which 17 were unambiguously 'different' by pHash and 5 fell in the ambiguous band. Visual judgment of the 5: 1 was different (CHAPTER 3 title card → THREE DAY RULE subsection card), 4 were same (sustained talking-head shots with only facial-expression changes). Conclusion: thresholds are right; ambiguous-pair count scales with sample density and amount of static talking-head content.

youtube-synthesizer Phase 1.5 e2e against MIqpvpNS5pI: produced 15 informational images (vs Phase 1's 19, vs t1k.5's '~6' estimate). The '~6' was a low estimate; each lord type in this video has 2-3 distinct illustrations (chokepoint: king-in-pass + castle-in-pass; surplus: farmer + caloric-battery + boat; junction: scales+barn + lord+map; specialist: NO-RIVER+cart + ADDS-VALUE) and the value-filter rule keeps all of them since each communicates something distinct. Audit strategy lesson from the pr-review-loop peer-skill series: when auditing a corpus of peer work (skills, papers, libraries, design references), measure value by per-insight LEVERAGE, not insight COUNT. Diminishing returns on new-idea volume is expected and fine. Confirmed empirically: audits 5-8 each yielded 1 ticket, but each ticket was substantively load-bearing (category prefix format, incremental round mode, deterministic pre-checks, codebase pattern detection). HOW TO APPLY: keep auditing until per-insight lift drops below the cost of the audit itself, not until ideas-per-audit hits zero. Equally, don't keep auditing past clear saturation just to feel thorough. One high-leverage insight from a 'small' audit can outweigh many low-leverage ones from a 'productive' one.

pr-review-loop development strategy: pick ONE feature, polish to upstream-merge quality, then move to next. The downstream is a direct fork of this repo with the user as co-maintainer; downstream has the same loop architecture, AGENT-REVIEWERS.md, multi-bot coordination, etc. — differences are business-audit features downstream-only. Implications: (1) merge-friendliness is NOT about avoiding architectural commitment — downstream already has it; (2) feature selection should weight 'highest-leverage for the shared architecture'; (3) polish discipline matters — docs, tests, config surface; (4) features that produce audit-friendly evidence (gx4 telemetry, ydy validator records, 5zk categories, vk0 permalinks) are bonus-valued because downstream's audit layer can consume them, even though the audit layer itself stays downstream. SUPERSEDES the earlier framing in this memory which assumed downstream had different architecture.

All tickets in the 4 pr-review-loop learning epics (gnp, 37s, 0cp, 4fw) and the cross-cutting filter framework (gx4) should be treated as SPIKES: experimental, exploratory work to improve the process. Each is a hypothesis to validate via real-world usage, not a commitment to ship permanently. Implications: (1) implement each in a way that's easy to disable or rip out, (2) prioritize measurability over polish, (3) accept that some will be reverted after data shows they don't earn their cost, (4) we eventually need a test harness — possibly a corpus of historical PRs replayable through different configs for offline A/B comparison. Captured this directive so future implementation work doesn't over-invest in any single approach prematurely.

ydy (independent validator pipeline) design LOCKED on 2026-05-15. Key decisions: (1) Validator runs after all agents return, before posting findings; per-finding parallel via Task tool. (2) Validator gets the finding text + diff context, NOT the flagger's name or rationale (independent context). (3) Output: VALID / INVALID / UNCERTAIN with one-line reason. (4) Filter: VALID → post normally; INVALID → drop silently; UNCERTAIN → post with [validator: uncertain] annotation. (5) Model selection: validator MIRRORS the flagger's model (opus-flagged → opus validator; sonnet-flagged → sonnet validator). This is the simplification of 0np's per-category tiering — the model itself encodes the tier. Half of validators end up sonnet (compliance agents) instead of blanket opus, which is the explicit user-stated goal 'not always using opus for reviewers'. (6) Configuration via # Configuration section: independent_validator: { enabled: true, skip_for: [], uncertain_action: post_with_annotation }. (7) Telemetry per gx4 spec: JSONL per-finding records with verdict, model, reason, posted-status. (8) Cross-family validator (devon-claude-skills-3m6 Phase C) is opt-in override that would override the mirror-flagger rule once it lands. Full spec lives in ydy ticket description under DESIGN (locked 2026-05-15) section.

youtube-synthesizer e2e: scene transcript boundaries cut mid-sentence are a real readability issue when sample timestamps don't align with sentence ends. The SKILL.md says reflow is allowed within a scene's transcript span; implementations should split paragraph breaks at sentence boundaries (period+space) before composing the scene body, not just dump the raw snippet concatenation. Without reflow, scene N's transcript can end like 'An ox eats roughly 10% of' with scene N+1 starting 'its cargo every 50 miles'.

NEVER push to main without explicit per-commit/per-batch authorization from the user. Every commit batch needs a separate 'commit and push' instruction. Auto mode does not blanket-authorize main pushes — it authorizes work, not destination. Specifically: when iterating on a feature in auto mode, commit locally as logical units, but NEVER 'git push' until the user explicitly says so AND has had the opportunity to review the changes (e.g., via a PR). Even for skill changes that we're dogfooding, the PR must come first; we don't bypass the review loop on the very loop we're modifying. Captured 2026-05-14 after I pushed 5 bfd commits straight to main without dogfooding the pr-review-loop on them — exactly the failure mode this memory exists to prevent. Recovery procedure when this happens: reset main back to the last user-authorized commit, move the unreviewed commits to a feature branch, open a PR, run the loop.

youtube-synthesizer Phase A rewrite — value-filter principle. Images appear in the wiki entry only when they communicate something the transcript words don't, OR when they help explain the narrative (even if multiple in a row). The 'scene' abstraction dissolves: no per-scene heading, no canonical-frame-per-scene. Instead: classify every candidate frame by KIND. Drop talking-head, blank/transition, pull-quote-text-cards, chapter-divider cards. Keep diagram, map, chart, code, ui, b-roll, animated-build-steps (all of them, not just the terminal), and hybrid-talking-head-with-info-overlay. Bisection trigger flips: bisect the GAP between consecutive keep-frames when it exceeds N seconds, looking for missed diagrams in talking-head-dominated stretches. Test target MIqpvpNS5pI ('The Ox That's Breaking Your Fantasy Map') was deliberately chosen for this failure mode — ~70% talking-head + visual-interest filler, ~30% load-bearing diagrams. Current implementation captured 19 scenes; correct implementation should produce ~6 informational images. Pairs with option D (decoupled prose body, images inline at timestamps).

youtube-synthesizer initial-sampling rule: ALWAYS include t=0.5 in the timestamp list. Phase A.2 lists this explicitly but the e2e test missed it (started at t=5) and lost the first ~5s of intro transcript. The video start frame is also commonly distinct (logo, title card) and should not be skipped. The transcript-boundary heuristic alone won't catch this — most transcripts start at t=0.04 or similar, which rounds away.

youtube-synthesizer title sanitization: SKILL.md Phase D.1 says 'strip anything that isn't [a-z0-9-]' (apostrophes/quotes disappear cleanly, leaving 'thats' from 'that's'), NOT replace-with-hyphen (which produces 'that-s' — visually awkward). The e2e build script used the wrong rule and produced '...that-s-breaking...' as the folder name. SKILL.md spec is correct; future implementations should follow the strip-don't-replace rule.
